hierarchy.lvl1: Description of Challenges / Obstacles Faced
Description of Challenges / Obstacles Faced

records.content:
One of the biggest challenges of our project was the scale of the data. The approximately 50 GB of raw MBO CSV files were too large for a basic local workflow, so we had to move beyond pandas and use Spark on an EMR cluster. This added significant infrastructure overhead. We had to manage S3, cluster setup, file movement, and long runtimes before we could begin the actual analysis. In addition, the Databento MBO data had its own conventions. We had to learn how to interpret event types, timestamps, flag bits, and start-of-day snapshot rows before we could build a useful feature table.

Performing EDA and modeling brought their own challenges. Every step had to account for the size of the dataset, which made exploration slower and model training more expensive. Simple changes often took a long time to rerun and evaluate. Furthermore, financial data is inherently difficult to model and forecast. Some patterns may be real, while others are just noise. That made it challenging to decide what features to use, how to tune the model, and how strongly to state our conclusions.

Another challenge was the project itself as a team effort. We were balancing classes with work, family, and daily life while trying to keep steady progress. We had teammates across different time zones and even different continents. That meant the project was not just about technical work. It was also about communication, teamwork, and empathy.

hierarchy.lvl1: Conclusions / Key Findings
Conclusions / Key Findings

records.content:
Across the four modeling notebooks, a consistent story emerged. The market-by-order (MBO) data contained useful signal for forecasting realized volatility over short time horizons. Each notebook approached the problem differently, but all pointed to the same broader conclusion.

The linear regression and ARIMA/GARCH notebooks helped clarify that result. The linear models showed that recent realized volatility was the key baseline signal, while the broader microstructure feature set still added useful information. ARIMA gave a weaker and less consistent result in its setting, suggesting that the signal was less stable in a return-based time-series framework. GARCH fit the volatility patterns much better and supported the idea of volatility clustering, where large moves tend to be followed by large moves and quiet periods tend to stay quiet.

The unsupervised learning results added another layer to the story. PCA showed that many of the features moved together in structured ways. Clustering methods grouped the contracts into natural asset classes, including Treasuries, energy, and FX. CUSUM changepoint analysis also identified specific dates and intraday windows when volatility expanded sharply across multiple contracts at once. This suggested that the data contained meaningful structure beyond random noise.

The XGBoost notebook reinforced the projectŌĆÖs main findings. It showed that order-flow and microstructure features improved volatility forecasts beyond a simple persistence baseline, especially at the longer horizon. Recent order activity, cancellations, and size-based features appeared consistently among the most useful predictors. Taken together, the results suggested that short-horizon realized volatility has a forecastable structure and that MBO microstructure features helped improve forecasts beyond recent volatility alone.

hierarchy.lvl1: CME Futures Volatility Prediction
CME Futures Volatility Prediction

records.content:
We want to study whether short-term order book activity can help explain and predict short-term market volatility
in CME futures data. We also want to explore whether volatility shows clear short-term patterns or regimes,
such as calm periods and more active periods. We will also use VIX future data (See UX1 data) as an additional data set as a measure of implied volatility
and whether that will also impact short-term market volatility.

hierarchy.lvl1: CME Futures Volatility Prediction
CME Futures Volatility Prediction

records.content:
We want to be able to:

Meaningful predictions based on futures market activity intraday. In particular, we hope to predict the next 60 seconds of volatility based on several factors, such as realized volatility, market liquidity, bid/offer, and implied volatility via VIX futures (See UX1 data as additional dataset).

Identify whether the data shows useful volatility patterns or regimes. This can be useful during times of interesting macroeconomic environments or announcements, as well as seasonality in general of data. For example, does volatility tend to be higher/lower during certain periods.

What types of models are you considering?

Baseline models, such as linear regression and logistic regression

Tree-based models, such as random forest, gradient boosting, or XGBoost

Clustering or regime-style methods to identify different volatility states

PCA analysis to reduce dimensionality

GARCH model / ARIMA models, any autoregressive models to help predict

hierarchy.lvl1: Build the MBO Feature Table
Build the MBO Feature Table

records.content:
This notebook connects to the AWS EMR Spark cluster, loads the raw MBO CSV files from Amazon S3, and wrangles them into a clean 1-second feature table for modeling.

Using the Databento schema and flag definitions, the notebook:

parses timestamps and decodes flag bits

filters snapshot and administrative rows

aggregates live events into 1-second buckets

engineers side, size, and trade-based price features

builds a dense 1-second session timeline

computes log returns, realized volatility targets, and rolling 60-second features

The final feature table is saved back to S3 as a Parquet dataset for later EDA and modeling.

# install packages
!pip uninstall -y dataproc-spark-connect pyspark
!pip install -q pyspark==3.5.6




# imports
import os
from getpass import getpass



# set up AWS credentials - so EMR can access S3

os.environ["AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID"] = getpass("AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID: ").strip()
os.environ["AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY"] = getpass("AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY: ").strip()
###Needed for AWS Academy###
#os.environ["AWS_SESSION_TOKEN"] = getpass("AWS_SESSION_TOKEN: ").strip()
###Update to your bucket Region###
os.environ["AWS_DEFAULT_REGION"] = "us-west-2"



# get client ip for AWS setup
!curl -s https://checkip.amazonaws.com



# Set to address of EMR stack
%set_env EMR_HOST=ec2-54-202-192-134.us-west-2.compute.amazonaws.com



from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
import os

# connect to the EMR Spark cluster
spark = SparkSession.builder \
  .appName("CIS-5450") \
  .remote("sc://{host}:15002".format(host=os.getenv('EMR_HOST'))) \
  .getOrCreate()




# use UTC to match the MBO market-data timestamps, not local clock
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.session.timeZone", "UTC")
# set partitions
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.shuffle.partitions", "100")



from pyspark.sql.types import StructType, StructField
from pyspark.sql.types import StringType, IntegerType, DoubleType, LongType


# define spark schema for MBO csv file using Databento schemas & data formats
# Note that we are loading timestamps as strings at first,
# since in metadata pretty_ts=true (ISO string) and timestamp includes nanoseconds
# Also, in metadata pretty_px=true so price is human-readable base 10
schema = StructType([
    StructField("ts_recv", StringType(), True),
    StructField("ts_event", StringType(), True),
    StructField("rtype", IntegerType(), True),
    StructField("publisher_id", IntegerType(), True),
    StructField("instrument_id", LongType(), True),
    StructField("action", StringType(), True),
    StructField("side", StringType(), True),
    StructField("price", DoubleType(), True),
    StructField("size", LongType(), True),
    StructField("channel_id", IntegerType(), True),
    StructField("order_id", LongType(), True),
    StructField("flags", IntegerType(), True),
    StructField("ts_in_delta", LongType(), True),
    StructField("sequence", LongType(), True),
    StructField("symbol", StringType(), True)
])



# path for MBO csv files stored in S3
###CHANGE AS NEEDED###
BUCKET = "cis545-mbo-data-596916982631-us-west-2-an"
S3_PREFIX = "raw_MBO/"
S3_PATH = f"s3://{BUCKET}/{S3_PREFIX}*.mbo.csv"



# read all MBO CSV files from S3 to spark df

sdf_raw = (
    spark.read
    .option("header", "true")
    .schema(schema)
    .csv(S3_PATH)
)



# quick inspection
print("Columns:")
print(sdf_raw.columns)

print("First 5 rows:")
sdf_raw.show(5, truncate=False)





# create view for spark SQL queries and make sdf_raw persistant
sdf_raw.createOrReplaceTempView("mbo_raw")




# get total row count before any processing
query = """
SELECT
    COUNT(*) AS total_rows
FROM mbo_raw
"""

spark.sql(query).show()



Raw CSV file: glbx-mdp3-20251201-20251215.mbo.csv - 43,405,386 rows, ~5.5 GB.

Raw CSV file: glbx-mdp3-20251216-20251231.mbo.csv - 47,863,703 rows, ~6.2 GB.

Raw CSV file: glbx-mdp3-20251115-20251130.mbo.csv - 135,013,176 rows, ~17.5 GB.

Raw CSV file: glbx-mdp3-20260101-20260115.mbo.csv - 156,197,111 rows, ~20.3 GB.

total rows for all MBO CSV files: 382,479,376

hierarchy.lvl1: Build the MBO Feature Table
Build the MBO Feature Table

records.content:
Drop null rows and inspect the key categorical columns
action, side, symbol, flags, rtype

According to Databento MBO schema:

rtype should be 160 for MBO records

action can represent Add, Cancel, Modify, cleaR book, Trade, Fill, or None

side indicates the side that initiates the event
and can be B (bid/buy), A (ask/sell), or N (no side specified)

flags is a bit field that carries message and data-quality information,
including snapshot-related indicators

# check number of rows for each action code
# action can be Add, Cancel, Modify, cleaR book, Trade, Fill, or None.
query = """
SELECT
    action,
    COUNT(*) AS action_count
FROM mbo_raw
GROUP BY
    action
ORDER BY
    action_count DESC
"""

spark.sql(query).show()



# count rows for each side value
# side can be Ask/sell, Bid/buy, or None when no side is specified
query = """
SELECT
    side,
    COUNT(*) AS side_count
FROM mbo_raw
GROUP BY
    side
ORDER BY
    side_count DESC
"""

spark.sql(query).show()



# count rows for each symbol
# ZT.c.0 = 2-Year Treasury
# ZF.c.0 = 5-Year Treasury
# ZN.c.0 = 10-Year Treasury
# ZB.c.0 = 30-Year Treasury Bond
# TN.c.0 = Ultra 10-Year Treasury
# UB.c.0 = Ultra Bond
# CL.c.0 = Crude Oil (WTI)
# BZ.c.0 = Brent Crude
# 6E.c.0 = Euro/USD FX

query = """
SELECT
    symbol,
    COUNT(*) AS symbol_count
FROM mbo_raw
GROUP BY
    symbol
ORDER BY
    symbol_count DESC
"""

spark.sql(query).show()



# count rows for each flag value
# flags is a bit field that may indicate snapshot, last record, bad ts_recv, etc.

query = """
SELECT
    flags,
    COUNT(*) AS count
FROM mbo_raw
GROUP BY
    flags
ORDER BY
    count DESC
"""

spark.sql(query).show()



# count rows for rtype value in MBO data
# rtype should be 160

query = """
SELECT
    rtype,
    COUNT(*) AS count
FROM mbo_raw
GROUP
    BY rtype
"""

spark.sql(query).show()



# clean null rows in key columns
query = """
SELECT
    *
FROM mbo_raw
WHERE
    rtype = 160
  AND ts_event IS NOT NULL
  AND flags IS NOT NULL
  AND action IS NOT NULL
  AND symbol IS NOT NULL
"""

mbo_clean = spark.sql(query)
mbo_clean.createOrReplaceTempView("mbo_clean")

hierarchy.lvl1: Build the MBO Feature Table
Build the MBO Feature Table

records.content:
The ts_event and ts_recv columns are loaded as strings because
pretty_ts=true in the metadata. This means we have ISO 8601
timestamp strings with nanosecond precision.

Convert timestamp string columns into Spark timestamps for use in time-based processing.
Spark stores timestamps with microsecond precision.
Not an issue since aggregating events into 1-second buckets.

# convert timestamp strings into Spark timestamps

query = """
SELECT
    *,
    TO_TIMESTAMP(ts_event) AS ts_event_parsed,
    TO_TIMESTAMP(ts_recv) AS ts_recv_parsed
FROM mbo_clean
"""

mbo_time_sdf = spark.sql(query)
mbo_time_sdf.createOrReplaceTempView("mbo_time")

mbo_time_sdf.show(5, truncate=False)



# confirm parsing of timestamps worked
query = """
SELECT
    COUNT(*) AS total_rows,
    SUM(CASE
        WHEN ts_event_parsed IS NULL
            THEN 1
            ELSE 0
        END) AS null_ts_event,
    SUM(CASE
        WHEN ts_recv_parsed IS NULL
            THEN 1
            ELSE 0
        END) AS null_ts_recv
FROM mbo_time
"""

spark.sql(query).show()

hierarchy.lvl1: Build the MBO Feature Table
Build the MBO Feature Table

records.content:
According to Databento MBO schema, the flags column is a bit field where a
single integer value can contain multiple indicators. Therefore, we will
decode the bits into separate columns before filtering the data.

is_last (128): last record in an event

is_tob (64): top-of-book message

is_snapshot (32): snapshot record

is_mbp (16): market-by-price message

is_bad_ts_recv (8): ts_recv is unreliable

is_maybe_bad_book (4): possible unrecoverable gap in the channel

is_publisher_specific (2): publisher-specific meaning

Remove is_snapshot rows to focus on live market activity.

# decode important Databento flag bits

query = """
SELECT
    *,
    CASE
        WHEN (flags & 128) != 0
            THEN 1
            ELSE 0
    END AS is_last,
    CASE
        WHEN (flags &  64) != 0
            THEN 1
            ELSE 0
    END AS is_tob,
    CASE
        WHEN (flags &  32) != 0
            THEN 1
            ELSE 0
    END AS is_snapshot,
    CASE
        WHEN (flags &  16) != 0
            THEN 1
            ELSE 0
    END AS is_mbp,
    CASE
        WHEN (flags &   8) != 0
            THEN 1
            ELSE 0
    END AS is_bad_ts_recv,
    CASE
        WHEN (flags &   4) != 0
            THEN 1
            ELSE 0
    END AS is_maybe_bad_book,
    CASE
        WHEN (flags &   2) != 0
            THEN 1
            ELSE 0
    END AS is_publisher_specific
FROM mbo_time
"""

flagged_sdf = spark.sql(query)
flagged_sdf.createOrReplaceTempView("mbo_flagged")



# check flags
query = """
SELECT
    SUM(is_last) AS last_rows,
    SUM(is_snapshot) AS snapshot_rows,
    SUM(is_bad_ts_recv) AS bad_ts_recv_rows,
    SUM(is_maybe_bad_book) AS maybe_bad_book_rows,
    SUM(is_publisher_specific) AS publisher_specific_rows,
    SUM(is_mbp) AS mbp_rows,
    SUM(is_tob) AS tob_rows
FROM mbo_flagged
"""

spark.sql(query).show()

hierarchy.lvl1: Build the MBO Feature Table
Build the MBO Feature Table

records.content:
Filter out rows that are not live market activity.

This includes:

snapshot rows identified by the Databento F_SNAPSHOT flag

R and N rows that are not part of live market activity

# keep only live market event rows
query = """
SELECT
    *
FROM mbo_flagged
WHERE
    is_snapshot = 0
AND
    action NOT IN ('R', 'N')
"""

filtered_sdf = spark.sql(query)
filtered_sdf.createOrReplaceTempView("mbo_filtered")



# sanity check
query = """
SELECT
    action,
    COUNT(*) AS action_count
FROM mbo_filtered
GROUP BY
    action
ORDER BY
    action_count DESC
"""

spark.sql(query).show()



# row count after filtering
query = """
SELECT
    COUNT(*) AS filtered_rows
FROM mbo_filtered
"""

spark.sql(query).show()

hierarchy.lvl1: Build the MBO Feature Table
Build the MBO Feature Table

records.content:
Assign each event for each symbol to a 1-second bucket, then
aggregate the events. Each row represents events for one symbol in one second.

The resulting table is a 1-second event summary with:

n_events - total events

n_add - number of add orders

n_cancel - number of cancel orders

n_modify - number of modify orders

n_trade - number of trade records (T)

n_fill - number of fill records (F)

n_add_bid - number of add orders on the bid side

n_add_ask - number of add orders on the ask side

n_cancel_bid - number of cancel orders on the bid side

n_cancel_ask - number of cancel orders on the ask side

n_modify_bid - number of modify orders on the bid side

n_modify_ask - number of modify orders on the ask side

n_trade_buy - number of trade records on the buy side

n_trade_sell - number of trade records on the sell side

n_trade_none - number of trade records with no side specified

trade_volume_buy - total trade size on the buy side

trade_volume_sell - total trade size on the sell side

trade_volume_none - total trade size with no side specified

trade_volume_total - total trade size

fill_volume_total - total fill size

# truncate event timestamps to the second so events occurring in the same second share one bucket
query = """
SELECT
    *,
    DATE_TRUNC('second', ts_event_parsed) AS second_bucket
FROM mbo_filtered
"""

# persist views when appropriate
from pyspark.storagelevel import StorageLevel

bucketed_sdf = spark.sql(query).persist(StorageLevel.MEMORY_AND_DISK)
bucketed_sdf.createOrReplaceTempView("mbo_bucketed")



# sanity check
query = """
SELECT
    ts_event,
    ts_event_parsed,
    second_bucket
FROM mbo_bucketed
LIMIT 10
"""

spark.sql(query).show(truncate=False)



# group by symbol and 1-second bucket, then aggregate events
# one row per contract per second that an event occurs

query = """
SELECT
    symbol,
    second_bucket,
    COUNT(*) AS n_events,
    SUM(CASE
        WHEN action = 'A'
            THEN 1
            ELSE 0
    END) AS n_add,
    SUM(CASE
        WHEN action = 'C'
            THEN 1
            ELSE 0
    END) AS n_cancel,
    SUM(CASE
        WHEN action = 'M'
            THEN 1
            ELSE 0
    END) AS n_modify,
    SUM(CASE
        WHEN action = 'T'
            THEN 1
            ELSE 0
    END) AS n_trade,
    SUM(CASE
        WHEN action = 'F'
            THEN 1
            ELSE 0
    END) AS n_fill,
    SUM(CASE
        WHEN action = 'A' AND side = 'B'
            THEN 1
            ELSE 0
    END) AS n_add_bid,
    SUM(CASE
        WHEN action = 'A' AND side = 'A'
            THEN 1
            ELSE 0
    END) AS n_add_ask,
    SUM(CASE
        WHEN action = 'C' AND side = 'B'
            THEN 1
            ELSE 0
    END) AS n_cancel_bid,
    SUM(CASE
        WHEN action = 'C' AND side = 'A'
            THEN 1
            ELSE 0
    END) AS n_cancel_ask,
    SUM(CASE
        WHEN action = 'M' AND side = 'B'
            THEN 1
            ELSE 0
    END) AS n_modify_bid,
    SUM(CASE
        WHEN action = 'M' AND side = 'A'
            THEN 1
            ELSE 0
    END) AS n_modify_ask,
    SUM(CASE
        WHEN action = 'T' AND side = 'B'
            THEN 1
            ELSE 0
    END) AS n_trade_buy,
    SUM(CASE
        WHEN action = 'T' AND side = 'A'
            THEN 1
            ELSE 0
    END) AS n_trade_sell,
    SUM(CASE
        WHEN action = 'T' AND side = 'N'
            THEN 1
            ELSE 0
    END) AS n_trade_none,
    SUM(CASE
        WHEN action = 'T' AND side = 'B'
            THEN size
            ELSE 0
    END) AS trade_volume_buy,
    SUM(CASE
        WHEN action = 'T' AND side = 'A'
            THEN size
            ELSE 0
    END) AS trade_volume_sell,
    SUM(CASE
        WHEN action = 'T' AND side = 'N'
            THEN size
            ELSE 0
    END) AS trade_volume_none,
    SUM(CASE
        WHEN action = 'T'
            THEN size
            ELSE 0
    END) AS trade_volume_total,
    SUM(CASE
        WHEN action = 'F'
            THEN size
            ELSE 0
    END) AS fill_volume_total
FROM mbo_bucketed
GROUP BY
    symbol,
    second_bucket
"""

sec_events_sdf = spark.sql(query).persist(StorageLevel.MEMORY_AND_DISK)
sec_events_sdf.createOrReplaceTempView("sec_events")



# sanity check

query = """
SELECT
    symbol,
    second_bucket,
    n_events,
    n_add,
    n_cancel,
    n_modify,
    n_trade,
    n_fill
FROM sec_events
WHERE
    symbol = '6E.c.0'
ORDER BY
    second_bucket
LIMIT 20
"""

spark.sql(query).show(truncate=False)



# count number of rows after grouping
query = """
SELECT
    COUNT(*) AS row_count
FROM sec_events
"""

spark.sql(query).show()



Number of rows after bucketing glbx-mdp3-20251201-20251215.mbo.csv:  2,337,510

Number of rows after bucketing glbx-mdp3-20251216-20251231.mbo.csv: 2,364,256

Number of rows after bucketing glbx-mdp3-20251115-20251130.mbo.csv: 3,909,795

hierarchy.lvl1: Build the MBO Feature Table
Build the MBO Feature Table

records.content:
Engineer additional 1-second features from side and size
for each symbol and second_bucket.

The side/size features include:

n_bid - number of bid-side events

n_ask - number of ask-side events

bid_size - total bid-side size

ask_size - total ask-side size

avg_size - average event size

sum_size - total event size

max_size - largest event size

The features are then joined with the sec_events table from the last step.

# aggregate side and size features for each symbol-second bucket

query = """
SELECT
    symbol,
    second_bucket,
    SUM(CASE
            WHEN side = 'B'
                THEN 1
                ELSE 0
        END) AS n_bid,
    SUM(CASE
            WHEN side = 'A'
                THEN 1
                ELSE 0
        END) AS n_ask,
    SUM(CASE
            WHEN side = 'B'
                THEN size
                ELSE 0
        END) AS bid_size,
    SUM(CASE
            WHEN side = 'A'
                THEN size
                ELSE 0
        END) AS ask_size,
    AVG(size) AS avg_size,
    SUM(size) AS sum_size,
    MAX(size) AS max_size
FROM mbo_bucketed
GROUP BY
    symbol,
    second_bucket
"""

side_size_sdf = spark.sql(query)
side_size_sdf.createOrReplaceTempView("side_size")



# sanity check
query = """
SELECT
    *
FROM side_size
LIMIT 10
"""

spark.sql(query).show()



# join side and size features to sec_events table

query = """
SELECT
    e.symbol,
    e.second_bucket,
    e.n_events,
    e.n_add,
    e.n_cancel,
    e.n_modify,
    e.n_trade,
    e.n_fill,
    e.n_add_bid,
    e.n_add_ask,
    e.n_cancel_bid,
    e.n_cancel_ask,
    e.n_modify_bid,
    e.n_modify_ask,
    e.n_trade_buy,
    e.n_trade_sell,
    e.n_trade_none,
    e.trade_volume_buy,
    e.trade_volume_sell,
    e.trade_volume_none,
    e.trade_volume_total,
    e.fill_volume_total,
    s.n_bid,
    s.n_ask,
    s.bid_size,
    s.ask_size,
    s.avg_size,
    s.sum_size,
    s.max_size
FROM sec_events e
JOIN side_size s
    ON e.symbol = s.symbol
    AND e.second_bucket = s.second_bucket
"""



features_sdf = spark.sql(query).persist(StorageLevel.MEMORY_AND_DISK)
features_sdf.createOrReplaceTempView("features")

SELECT
    COUNT(*) AS n_rows
FROM features
"""

hierarchy.lvl2: Create trade-only last price column and join with feature table

records.content:
Create a 1-second price series using only trade rows,
i.e. action = 'T', so it is based on executed transactions only.

For each symbol and second_bucket,
select the last trade price using a deterministic ordering.

Then use a LEFT JOIN to combine the feature table with the
trade-price table so all feature rows are kept.

# get last trade price for each symbol per second from mbo_filtered

query = """
WITH trades AS (
    SELECT
        symbol,
        DATE_TRUNC('second', ts_event_parsed) AS second_bucket,
        price,
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
            PARTITION BY
                symbol,
                DATE_TRUNC('second', ts_event_parsed)
            ORDER BY
                ts_event DESC,
                channel_id DESC,
                sequence DESC,
                order_id DESC
        ) AS row_num
    FROM mbo_filtered
    WHERE
        action = 'T'
        AND price IS NOT NULL
)
SELECT
    symbol,
    second_bucket,
    price AS last_price
FROM trades
WHERE
    row_num = 1
"""

last_price_sdf = spark.sql(query)
last_price_sdf.createOrReplaceTempView("last_price")



# join last_price into the feature table

query = """
SELECT
    f.symbol,
    f.second_bucket,
    f.n_events,
    f.n_add,
    f.n_cancel,
    f.n_modify,
    f.n_trade,
    f.n_fill,
    f.n_add_bid,
    f.n_add_ask,
    f.n_cancel_bid,
    f.n_cancel_ask,
    f.n_modify_bid,
    f.n_modify_ask,
    f.n_trade_buy,
    f.n_trade_sell,
    f.n_trade_none,
    f.trade_volume_buy,
    f.trade_volume_sell,
    f.trade_volume_none,
    f.trade_volume_total,
    f.fill_volume_total,
    f.n_bid,
    f.n_ask,
    f.bid_size,
    f.ask_size,
    f.avg_size,
    f.sum_size,
    f.max_size,
    p.last_price
FROM features f
LEFT JOIN last_price p
    ON f.symbol = p.symbol
    AND f.second_bucket = p.second_bucket
"""


features_last_price_sdf = spark.sql(query).persist(StorageLevel.MEMORY_AND_DISK)
features_last_price_sdf.createOrReplaceTempView("features_last_price")



# confirm feature row count

query = """
SELECT
    COUNT(*) AS feature_rows
FROM features_last_price
"""

spark.sql(query).show()



# May need to unpersist old views depending on memory constraints of your compute cluster
#features_sdf.unpersist()

hierarchy.lvl1: Build the MBO Feature Table
Build the MBO Feature Table

records.content:
The combined features table is sparse because many seconds have no events.This is a problem for 60-second clock-time calculations, since we want one row for every second.

To fix this, we assign each row to a CME trading session and build a full 1-second timeline
for each observed symbol-session using the standard-time (CST-based) UTC session schedule:

starts at 23:00:00 UTC on the prior date

ends at 21:59:59 UTC on the session date

excludes the 22:00ŌĆō22:59 UTC maintenance hour

Steps include:

assign session_date to each sparse row

identify all symbol-session pairs

generate one row for every second in each session

join the features table onto that timeline

fill event and size features with zeros, while leaving last_price as null for now

remove holiday-affected and incomplete sessions

This gives a full second-by-second table for each observed symbol-session.

# assign CME session_date to each row in features table
# 23:00 hour belongs to the next session date
# 00:00-21:59 belongs to the same session date
# 22:00 hour is excluded as the maintenance window

query = """
SELECT
    *,
    CASE
        WHEN HOUR(second_bucket) = 23 THEN DATE_ADD(TO_DATE(second_bucket), 1)
        WHEN HOUR(second_bucket) BETWEEN 0 AND 21 THEN TO_DATE(second_bucket)
        ELSE NULL
    END AS session_date
FROM features_last_price
WHERE
    HOUR(second_bucket) != 22
"""

session_times_sdf = spark.sql(query)
session_times_sdf.createOrReplaceTempView("session_times")



# keep only weekday session dates
# assign official session start and end times

query = """
WITH session_days AS (
    SELECT DISTINCT
        symbol,
        session_date
    FROM session_times
    WHERE
        session_date IS NOT NULL
        AND DAYOFWEEK(session_date) BETWEEN 2 AND 6
)
SELECT
    symbol,
    session_date,
    TO_TIMESTAMP(CONCAT(CAST(DATE_SUB(session_date, 1) AS STRING), ' 23:00:00')) AS session_start,
    TO_TIMESTAMP(CONCAT(CAST(session_date AS STRING), ' 21:59:59')) AS session_end
FROM session_days
"""

sessions_sdf = spark.sql(query)
sessions_sdf.createOrReplaceTempView("sessions")



# create one row for every second in every observed session for each symbol

query = """
SELECT
    symbol,
    session_date,
    EXPLODE(
        SEQUENCE(
            session_start,
            session_end,
            INTERVAL 1 SECOND
        )
    ) AS second_bucket
FROM sessions
"""

session_secs_sdf = spark.sql(query).persist(StorageLevel.MEMORY_AND_DISK)
session_secs_sdf.createOrReplaceTempView("session_secs")



# join features table onto the dense timeline
# fill activity and size columns with 0
# keep last_price as null for now

query = """
SELECT
    s.symbol,
    s.session_date,
    s.second_bucket,
    COALESCE(f.n_events, 0) AS n_events,
    COALESCE(f.n_add, 0) AS n_add,
    COALESCE(f.n_cancel, 0) AS n_cancel,
    COALESCE(f.n_modify, 0) AS n_modify,
    COALESCE(f.n_trade, 0) AS n_trade,
    COALESCE(f.n_fill, 0) AS n_fill,
    COALESCE(f.n_add_bid, 0) AS n_add_bid,
    COALESCE(f.n_add_ask, 0) AS n_add_ask,
    COALESCE(f.n_cancel_bid, 0) AS n_cancel_bid,
    COALESCE(f.n_cancel_ask, 0) AS n_cancel_ask,
    COALESCE(f.n_modify_bid, 0) AS n_modify_bid,
    COALESCE(f.n_modify_ask, 0) AS n_modify_ask,
    COALESCE(f.n_trade_buy, 0) AS n_trade_buy,
    COALESCE(f.n_trade_sell, 0) AS n_trade_sell,
    COALESCE(f.n_trade_none, 0) AS n_trade_none,
    COALESCE(f.trade_volume_buy, 0) AS trade_volume_buy,
    COALESCE(f.trade_volume_sell, 0) AS trade_volume_sell,
    COALESCE(f.trade_volume_none, 0) AS trade_volume_none,
    COALESCE(f.trade_volume_total, 0) AS trade_volume_total,
    COALESCE(f.fill_volume_total, 0) AS fill_volume_total,
    COALESCE(f.n_bid, 0) AS n_bid,
    COALESCE(f.n_ask, 0) AS n_ask,
    COALESCE(f.bid_size, 0) AS bid_size,
    COALESCE(f.ask_size, 0) AS ask_size,
    COALESCE(f.avg_size, 0) AS avg_size,
    COALESCE(f.sum_size, 0) AS sum_size,
    COALESCE(f.max_size, 0) AS max_size,
    f.last_price
FROM session_secs s
LEFT JOIN features_last_price f
    ON s.symbol = f.symbol
    AND s.second_bucket = f.second_bucket
"""


features_dense_sdf = spark.sql(query).persist(StorageLevel.MEMORY_AND_DISK)
features_dense_sdf.createOrReplaceTempView("features_dense")



# row count

query = """
SELECT
    COUNT(*) AS rows
FROM features_dense
"""

spark.sql(query).show(truncate=False)



# May need to unpersist old views depending on memory constraints of your compute cluster
#features_last_price_sdf.unpersist()

hierarchy.lvl2: Forward-fill trade price and create the previous-price column

hierarchy.lvl1: Build the MBO Feature Table
hierarchy.lvl2: Forward-fill trade price and create the previous-price column

records.content:
The last_price column only has values in rows where an executed trade occurred.For seconds with no executed trade, we forward-fill the most recent observed trade
price within each symbol and session_date to create filled_price.

We also create prev_price by taking the filled trade price from the previous second.

These columns are needed to compute the 1-second log return:

filled_price = current secondŌĆÖs trade price

prev_price = previous secondŌĆÖs trade price

# forward-fill the last observed trade price within each symbol and session_date

query = """
SELECT
    *,
    LAST_VALUE(last_price, TRUE)
        OVER (
            PARTITION BY
                symbol,
                session_date
            ORDER BY
                second_bucket
            ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
    ) AS filled_price
FROM features_dense
"""

features_fill_sdf = spark.sql(query)
features_fill_sdf.createOrReplaceTempView("features_fill")



# get the previous second's filled trade price

query = """
SELECT
    *,
    LAG(filled_price) OVER (
        PARTITION BY
            symbol,
            session_date
        ORDER BY
            second_bucket
    ) AS prev_price
FROM features_fill
"""

features_lag_sdf = spark.sql(query)
features_lag_sdf.createOrReplaceTempView("features_lag")

hierarchy.lvl1: Build the MBO Feature Table
Build the MBO Feature Table

records.content:
Compute the 1-second log return using the filled current price and the previous secondŌĆÖs price:

log_return = ln(filled_price / prev_price)

The first row for each symbol by session_date partition has no previous price, so its log return is NULL.

We use log returns rather than simple returns because they are additive over
time, symmetric around zero, and the standard basis for realized volatility.

# compute the 1-second log return from the filled price series

query = """
SELECT
    *,
    CASE
        WHEN
            filled_price > 0
            AND prev_price > 0
                THEN LN(filled_price / prev_price)
                ELSE NULL
    END AS log_return
FROM features_lag
"""

features_log_sdf = spark.sql(query)
features_log_sdf.createOrReplaceTempView("features_log")

hierarchy.lvl1: Build the MBO Feature Table
hierarchy.lvl2: Compute past 60-second, future 60-second, and future 300-second realized volatility

hierarchy.lvl1: Build the MBO Feature Table
hierarchy.lvl2: Compute past 60-second, future 60-second, and future 300-second realized volatility

records.content:
Use the 1-second log returns to compute realized volatility over rolling windows.

For each row, we create:

rv_past_60: realized volatility over the previous 60 seconds, including the current second

rv_next_60: realized volatility over the next 60 seconds  (label for future models)

rv_next_300: realized volatility over the next 300 seconds, or 5 minutes (label for future models)

All are computed as the square root of the sum of squared 1-second log returns within the corresponding window.

Seconds with no price change have log_return = 0, so they contribute zero to realized volatility.

All calculations stay within each partition. Therefore, we focus on within-session short-horizon volatility by
preventing windows from crossing CME maintenance breaks or between-session gaps.


# compute realized volatility over the past 60 seconds,
# next 60 seconds, and next 5 minutes

query = """
SELECT
    *,
    SQRT(SUM(POWER(COALESCE(log_return, 0.0), 2)) OVER (
        PARTITION BY
            symbol,
            session_date
        ORDER BY
            second_bucket
        ROWS BETWEEN 59 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
    )) AS rv_past_60,
    SQRT(SUM(POWER(COALESCE(log_return, 0.0), 2)) OVER (
        PARTITION BY
            symbol,
            session_date
        ORDER BY
            second_bucket
        ROWS BETWEEN 1 FOLLOWING AND 60 FOLLOWING
    )) AS rv_next_60,
    SQRT(SUM(POWER(COALESCE(log_return, 0.0), 2)) OVER (
        PARTITION BY
            symbol,
            session_date
        ORDER BY
            second_bucket
        ROWS BETWEEN 1 FOLLOWING AND 300 FOLLOWING
    )) AS rv_next_300
FROM features_log
"""

features_rv_sdf = spark.sql(query).persist(StorageLevel.MEMORY_AND_DISK)
features_rv_sdf.createOrReplaceTempView("features_rv")

hierarchy.lvl1: Build the MBO Feature Table
hierarchy.lvl2: Compute past 60-second, future 60-second, and future 300-second realized volatility

hierarchy.lvl1: Build the MBO Feature Table
hierarchy.lvl2: Compute past 60-second, future 60-second, and future 300-second realized volatility

records.content:
Compute rolling 60-second features within each symbol by session_date partition, including:

rolling order book activity (n_events_60, n_add_60, n_cancel_60, n_modify_60, n_trade_60, n_fill_60)

rolling size / volume (sum_size_60)

rolling imbalance (order_imbalance_60, size_imbalance_60)

rolling return dispersion (ret_std_60), i.e. standard deviation of 1-second log returns over the past 60 seconds

Drop:

rows without a full 60-second lookback

rows without a valid future 60-second target

rows without a valid future 300-second target

We will use 60-second rolling features to help predict future 60-second and 5-minute realized volatility.

# create rolling 60-second features
# keep only rows with a full 60-second lookback,
# full 60-second future horizon, and full 5-minute future horizon

query = """
WITH rolling AS (
    SELECT
        *,
        SUM(n_events) OVER past_window AS n_events_60,
        SUM(n_add) OVER past_window AS n_add_60,
        SUM(n_cancel) OVER past_window AS n_cancel_60,
        SUM(n_modify) OVER past_window AS n_modify_60,
        SUM(n_trade) OVER past_window AS n_trade_60,
        SUM(n_fill) OVER past_window AS n_fill_60,
        SUM(sum_size) OVER past_window AS sum_size_60,
        SUM(n_bid - n_ask) OVER past_window AS order_imbalance_60,
        SUM(bid_size - ask_size) OVER past_window AS size_imbalance_60,
        STDDEV_POP(COALESCE(log_return, 0.0)) OVER past_window AS ret_std_60,
        COUNT(*) OVER past_window AS n_past_60,
        COUNT(*) OVER future_window_60 AS n_next_60,
        COUNT(*) OVER future_window_300 AS n_next_300
    FROM features_rv
    WINDOW
        past_window AS (
            PARTITION BY
                symbol,
                session_date
            ORDER BY
                second_bucket
            ROWS BETWEEN 59 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
        ),
        future_window_60 AS (
            PARTITION BY
                symbol,
                session_date
            ORDER BY
                second_bucket
            ROWS BETWEEN 1 FOLLOWING AND 60 FOLLOWING
        ),
        future_window_300 AS (
            PARTITION BY
                symbol,
                session_date
            ORDER BY
                second_bucket
            ROWS BETWEEN 1 FOLLOWING AND 300 FOLLOWING
        )
)
SELECT
    *
FROM rolling
WHERE
    n_past_60 = 60
    AND n_next_60 = 60
    AND n_next_300 = 300
    AND filled_price IS NOT NULL
    AND prev_price IS NOT NULL
    AND log_return IS NOT NULL
"""

features_rolling_sdf = spark.sql(query).persist(StorageLevel.MEMORY_AND_DISK)
features_rolling_sdf.createOrReplaceTempView("features_rolling")



# final inspection before saving
query = """
SELECT
    *
FROM features_rolling
WHERE
    symbol = '6E.c.0'
    AND filled_price IS NOT NULL
    AND prev_price IS NOT NULL
    AND log_return IS NOT NULL
    AND rv_past_60 > 0
    AND rv_next_60 > 0
    AND rv_next_300 > 0
    AND n_trade_60 > 0
ORDER BY
  session_date,
  second_bucket
LIMIT 100
"""

spark.sql(query).show(truncate=False)





# move both target columns to the end
target_cols = ["rv_next_60", "rv_next_300"]

other_cols = [c for c in features_rolling_sdf.columns if c not in target_cols]
features_final_sdf = features_rolling_sdf.select(*other_cols, *target_cols)




# drop helper columns
features_final_sdf = features_final_sdf.drop("n_past_60", "n_next_60", "n_next_300")




print(features_final_sdf.columns)



#persist and materialize final table while getting final row count before write to S3

features_final_sdf = features_final_sdf.persist(StorageLevel.MEMORY_AND_DISK)
features_final_sdf.count()

Dataset can then be loaded either:

# save parquet dataset partitioned by symbols
features_final_sdf.write.mode("overwrite").partitionBy("symbol").parquet(S3_OUT)

records.content:
Follows the analytical pattern from Python for Data Analysis (McKinney, Ch. 5, 9, 10, 11):
correlation ŌåÆ grouped aggregations ŌåÆ rolling/resampling ŌåÆ financial time series patterns.

Answers the questions a modeling notebook needs:

Which features correlate with the RV targets?

Is rv_past_60 a useful predictor of rv_next_60?

Do symbols share RV dynamics, or do we need per-symbol models?

What intraday patterns exist that could be features?

Which sessions should be excluded before splitting train/test?

Run EDA_basic.ipynb first, then use this notebook to inform modeling decisions.

SYMBOLS = ["ZT.c.0", "ZF.c.0", "ZN.c.0", "ZB.c.0", "TN.c.0", "UB.c.0",
           "CL.c.0", "BZ.c.0", "6E.c.0"]

df = pd.read_parquet(DATA_PATH, filters=[("symbol", "in", SYMBOLS)])
df["second_bucket"] = pd.to_datetime(df["second_bucket"])
df["session_date"]  = pd.to_datetime(df["session_date"]).dt.date

print(f"Rows    : {len(df):,}")
print(f"Symbols : {sorted(df['symbol'].unique())}")
print(f"Date range: {df['session_date'].min()} ŌåÆ {df['session_date'].max()}")

records.content:
holiday-period sessions as having abnormal activity.

FULL_SESSION_SECS = 82800 - 59 - 300  # 82,441 after trimming

sess_stats = (
    df.groupby(["symbol", "session_date"])
    .agg(
        n_seconds=("second_bucket", "count"),
        n_trades=("n_trade", "sum"),
        median_events=("n_events", "median"),
    )
    .reset_index()
)

# flag sessions with fewer than 80% of expected seconds
sess_stats["is_short"] = sess_stats["n_seconds"] < FULL_SESSION_SECS * 0.8

short_sessions = sess_stats[sess_stats["is_short"]].sort_values("n_seconds")
print(f"Anomalous sessions detected: {short_sessions['session_date'].nunique()} unique dates")
print(short_sessions[["session_date", "symbol", "n_seconds", "n_trades"]].to_string())

# recommended dates to exclude
EXCLUDE_DATES = sorted(short_sessions["session_date"].unique().tolist())
# also exclude the known final session
import datetime
final_session = datetime.date(2026, 1, 16)
if final_session not in EXCLUDE_DATES:
    EXCLUDE_DATES.append(final_session)
print(f"\nDates to exclude: {EXCLUDE_DATES}")



# clean dataset for modeling analysis
df_clean = df[~df["session_date"].isin(EXCLUDE_DATES)].copy()
rv_clean  = df_clean[df_clean["rv_past_60"] > 0].copy()

print(f"Rows after excluding anomalous sessions : {len(df_clean):,}")
print(f"Rows with non-zero rv_past_60           : {len(rv_clean):,}")

records.content:
rv_cols  = [c for c in ["rv_past_60", "rv_next_60", "rv_next_300"] if c in df.columns]

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, len(rv_cols), figsize=(5 * len(rv_cols), 4))
if len(rv_cols) == 1:
    axes = [axes]
for ax, col in zip(axes, rv_cols):
    vals = rv_clean[col].dropna()
    ax.hist(np.log1p(vals), bins=80, edgecolor="none", alpha=0.85)
    ax.set_title(f"log1p({col})")
    ax.set_xlabel("log1p(RV)")
    ax.set_ylabel("Count")
    ax.text(0.97, 0.95, f"skew={vals.skew():.2f}", transform=ax.transAxes,
            ha="right", va="top", fontsize=8)
plt.suptitle("RV target distributions (log scale) ŌĆö clean sessions only")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

print("Modeling note: RV is right-skewed. Consider log1p transform or Huber loss.")





# median RV by symbol ŌĆö informs whether a pooled or per-symbol model is needed
rv_by_sym = rv_clean.groupby("symbol")[rv_cols].median().sort_values("rv_past_60", ascending=False)
print("Median RV by symbol:")
display(rv_by_sym)

rv_by_sym.plot(kind="bar", figsize=(11, 4), edgecolor="none")
plt.title("Median realized volatility by symbol (clean sessions)")
plt.ylabel("Median RV")
plt.xticks(rotation=30)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

print("\nModeling note: RV levels differ significantly across symbols.")
print("Consider per-symbol models or normalizing RV within symbol.")

records.content:
Is past RV a useful predictor of future RV? This is the baseline any model must beat.

# corr() per symbol
corr_past_next = (
    rv_clean.groupby("symbol")[["rv_past_60", "rv_next_60"]]
    .corr()
    .unstack()["rv_past_60"]["rv_next_60"]
    .rename("pearson_r")
    .sort_values(ascending=False)
    .round(4)
)
print("Pearson corr(rv_past_60, rv_next_60) per symbol:")
display(corr_past_next)

# corrwith() ŌĆö correlation of one Series against many columns
rv_features = rv_clean[["rv_past_60", "rv_next_60", "rv_next_300"]].dropna()
print("\ncorrwith(rv_next_60) across RV columns:")
print(rv_features.corrwith(rv_features["rv_next_60"]).round(4))

print("\nModeling note: rv_past_60 is a strong baseline predictor.")
print("Any model should outperform simply predicting rv_next_60 = rv_past_60.")







# scatter plot: past vs future RV (sample)
sample = rv_clean.groupby("symbol", group_keys=False).apply(
    lambda g: g.sample(min(3000, len(g)), random_state=42)

records.content:
#Overview

This notebook continues from the EDA, focusing on the unsupervised learning of the inherent structures within the identified features and targets (rv_next_60, rv_next_300). This part leverages on the Principal Component Analysis (PCA), T-distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding (t-SNE), Hierarchical Clustering, Cumulative Sum (CUSUM) statistics and  Singular Spectrum Analysis (SSA).
The plan is as follows.

Setup, load data, preprosessing and train/val/test spilt

Feature standardization

PCA on the multivariate time series, inspecting the global collinearity among the features, plot the cumulative explained variance and identify the top components.

t-SNE to inspect the non-linear clustering as suggested in the cross-symbol correlation from EDA and potential asset class clustering.

Hierarchical clustering and  dendrogram to inspect the similarity among the symbols

CUSUM analysis to illustrate the daily change points, further zoom into the intraday change points by 5s resampled in the volatility expansion period

Apply SSA to the top features identified in PCA and targets (rv_next_60, rv_next_300) in train dataset, reconstruct the validation dataset, forecast and evaluate the test dataset.

records.content:
# holiday session dates flagged in EDA_advanced
EXCLUDE_DATES = [
    '2025-11-28',  # Thanksgiving (US)
    '2025-12-24',  # Christmas Eve
    '2025-12-25',  # Christmas
    '2025-12-31',  # New Year's Eve
    '2026-01-01',  # New Year's Day
    '2026-01-16',  # final session (abnormal activity)
]


sdf_clean = sdf \
    .filter(~F.col('session_date').cast('string').isin(EXCLUDE_DATES)) \
    .filter(F.col('rv_past_60') > 0) \
    .filter(F.col('rv_next_60') > 0) \
    .filter(F.col('rv_next_60').isNotNull()) \
    .filter(F.col('rv_next_300').isNotNull())

    .withColumn('hour_utc',         F.hour('second_bucket').cast(DoubleType())) \

# 70/15/15 Split

dates = [
    r[0] for r in
    sdf_clean.select('session_date').distinct().orderBy('session_date').collect()
]
n = len(dates)

train_end = dates[int(n * 0.70) - 1]
val_end   = dates[int(n * 0.85) - 1]
test_end  = dates[-1]

train_df = sdf_clean.filter(F.col('session_date') <= train_end)
val_df   = sdf_clean.filter((F.col('session_date') > train_end) & (F.col('session_date') <= val_end))
test_df  = sdf_clean.filter(F.col('session_date') > val_end)

hierarchy.lvl1: Top 5 Features for Each Asset Class
Top 5 Features for Each Asset Class

records.content:
As suggested in the cross-symbol correlation from EDA and potential asset class clustering, here we group the 9 futures into 3 asset classes: EURO FX, Crude Oil, and US Treasury. Within each group, we calculate the mean of each PCA component and project back to the feature space.

groups = {
    'EURO FX': ['6E.c.0'],
    'Crude Oil': ['CL.c.0', 'BZ.c.0'],
    'US Treasury': ['ZT.c.0','ZF.c.0','ZN.c.0','ZB.c.0','TN.c.0','UB.c.0']
}

# Principal components matrix

components_matrix = pca_model.pc.toArray()

print("Top 5 contributing features and Average PCA Scores for each group:\n")

def get_vector_element(vector, index):
    if vector is None:
        return None
    return float(vector[index])

get_vector_element_udf = udf(get_vector_element, DoubleType())

for group_name, symbols in groups.items():
    print(f"------ {group_name} ------\n")

    group_df = train_pca.filter(F.col('symbol').isin(symbols))

    pca_component_cols = [f"pc_{i}" for i in range(len(FEATURE_COLS))]
    for i in range(len(FEATURE_COLS)):
        group_df = group_df.withColumn(f"pc_{i}", get_vector_element_udf(F.col("pca_features"), F.lit(i)))

    # Calculate the mean of each PCA component for the group

    avg_pca_scores_row = group_df.select([F.avg(c).alias(c) for c in pca_component_cols]).first()

    # Extract the mean scores

    if avg_pca_scores_row:

        avg_pca_scores = np.array([avg_pca_scores_row[c] for c in pca_component_cols])
    else:
        avg_pca_scores = np.zeros(len(FEATURE_COLS))

    # Project the average PCA scores back to the original feature space

    feature_contributions = np.dot(components_matrix, avg_pca_scores)

    feature_contributions_series = pd.Series(feature_contributions, index=FEATURE_COLS)

    # Get the top 5 features by absolute contribution

    top_5_features = feature_contributions_series.abs().sort_values(ascending=False).head(5)

    top_5_df = pd.DataFrame({'Contribution': top_5_features.values}, index=top_5_features.index)
    top_5_df = top_5_df.reset_index()
    top_5_df = top_5_df.rename(columns={'index': 'Feature'}) #

    print(top_5_df.to_string(index=False))
    print("\n")



#t-SNE Visualization

Continue from the asset class clustering hypothesis, we further explore the non-linear struture and local clustering in this high-dimensional data.

By applying t-SNE on the first 5 principal components, we look for the local clusters that might not be apparent in PCA linear projection.

from sklearn.manifold import TSNE

        return None
    return vector.toArray()[:n].tolist()

extract_pca_features_udf = udf(lambda v: extract_first_n_elements(v, 5), ArrayType(DoubleType()))

# Extracted 5 PCA features and symbol
tsne_input_sdf = train_pca.withColumn(
    "pca_features_5",
    extract_pca_features_udf(train_pca["pca_features"])
).select("pca_features_5", "symbol")

The choices of the selected features and distance metric will result in different clustering. Here we selected the features based on (1) top features identified; (2) less instrument-specific features

from scipy.spatial.distance import pdist, squareform
from scipy.cluster import hierarchy
from scipy.cluster.hierarchy import linkage



# Selected features for clustering
SELECTED_FEATURES = ['rv_past_60', 'ret_std_60', 'hour_utc','size_imbalance_60', 'minute_of_hour','n_modify_60']

# Aggregate features for each symbol
features_for_clustering_sdf = sdf_clean.groupBy('symbol').agg(
    *[F.mean(col).alias(col) for col in SELECTED_FEATURES]
)

features_df_pandas = features_for_clustering_sdf.toPandas()

features_df = features_df_pandas.set_index('symbol')

# pdist computes pairwise distances
row_dist = np.log(pdist(features_df, metric='euclidean'))

row_clusters = linkage(row_dist, method='complete')

# Plot the dendrogram
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
dn = hierarchy.dendrogram(row_clusters,
                           labels=features_df.index.to_list(),
                           leaf_rotation=90,
                           leaf_font_size=10,
                           show_contracted=True
                          )
plt.title('Hierarchical Clustering Dendrogram of 9 Futures')
plt.xlabel('Symbol')
plt.ylabel('Log of Euclidean Distance')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Hierarchical Clustering

records.content:
The Energy & Currency Cluster (CL, 6E, BZ): this clustering supports the ŌĆ£PetrodollarŌĆØ anchor. The closer link between CL(WTI) and 6E(Euro) might suggest the geopolitical divergence (the Brent ŌĆ£war premiumŌĆØ) in 2026.

The Interest Rate/Bond Cluster (ZN, ZB, UB): These Treasury futures (10-year, 30-year, and Ultra Bonds)cluster together as they are all driven by the yield curve and inflation expectations.

The ŌĆ£OutlierŌĆØ: ZT (2-Year Note) only joins the rest of the group at the very top. This suggests that during Nov 2025 - Jan 2026, the short-end of the yield curve (ZT) was decoupled from the long-end and the energy/FX sectors.

The hierarchical clustering is subejct to the selected features and distance metric. One can inspect the hierarchical links based on the specific use case. E.g. portfolio construction or hedging basket etc.

We continue the SSA analysis based on the asset class clustering which is not strongly against by this hierarchical clustering result.

hierarchy.lvl1: CUSUM Statistics
CUSUM Statistics

records.content:
The CUSUM (Cumulative Sum) statistic is often used to detect subtle shifts and change points in a process over time.

Here we used CUSUM to investigate the target rv_next_60 (a finer scale than ŌĆśrv_next_300ŌĆÖ) on a daily frequency first, identified the most volatile date and zoomed into the specific date on a 5-seconds resampling to identify the change points.

hierarchy.lvl1: CUSUM Statistics
CUSUM Statistics

records.content:
# helper function

def cusum_daily(spark_df, symbol_to_analyze, feature_col, sample_fraction=0.5):

    """
    Performs daily resampled CUSUM analysis.

    Args:
        spark_df (pyspark.sql.DataFrame): The input Spark DataFrame (sdf_clean).
        symbol_to_analyze (str): The financial symbol to analyze.
        feature_col (str): The column to perform CUSUM analysis on.
        sample_fraction (float): Fraction of data to sample before converting to Pandas.

    Returns:
        tuple: (time_index, cusum_positive, cusum_negative, change_points, h_threshold, series_to_analyze)
    """
    single_symbol_sdf_cusum = (
        spark_df.filter(F.col('symbol') == symbol_to_analyze)
                .select(F.col('second_bucket').cast('string').alias('second_bucket_str'), feature_col)
                .orderBy('second_bucket_str')
    )


    single_symbol_sdf_cusum = single_symbol_sdf_cusum.sample(False, fraction=sample_fraction, seed=42)


    single_symbol_pd_cusum = single_symbol_sdf_cusum.toPandas()

    if single_symbol_pd_cusum.empty:
        return pd.Series(), np.array([]), np.array([]), [], 0.0, np.array([])

    single_symbol_pd_cusum['second_bucket'] = pd.to_datetime(single_symbol_pd_cusum['second_bucket_str'])
    single_symbol_pd_cusum = single_symbol_pd_cusum.drop(columns=['second_bucket_str'])

    single_symbol_pd_cusum_filtered = single_symbol_pd_cusum.dropna(subset=[feature_col])

    # Resample to daily frequency

    daily_resampled_df = (
        single_symbol_pd_cusum_filtered.set_index('second_bucket')
                                         .resample('D')[feature_col].sum()
                                         .reset_index()
    )
    daily_resampled_df = daily_resampled_df.dropna(subset=[feature_col])

    series_to_analyze = daily_resampled_df[feature_col].values
    time_index = daily_resampled_df['second_bucket']

    if len(series_to_analyze) == 0:
        return time_index, np.array([]), np.array([]), [], 0.0, np.array([])

    mean_series = np.mean(series_to_analyze)
    std_series = np.std(series_to_analyze)

    if std_series == 0:
        return time_index, np.zeros_like(series_to_analyze), np.zeros_like(series_to_analyze), [], 0.0, series_to_analyze

    k = 0.5 * std_series # Slack
    h = 4 * std_series   # Threshold

    cusum_positive = np.zeros_like(series_to_analyze, dtype=float)
    cusum_negative = np.zeros_like(series_to_analyze, dtype=float)

    S_h = 0  # for upward shift
    S_l = 0  # for downward shift
    change_points = []

    for i, x in enumerate(series_to_analyze):
        S_h = max(0, S_h + (x - mean_series - k))
        S_l = min(0, S_l + (x - mean_series + k))
        cusum_positive[i] = S_h
        cusum_negative[i] = S_l
        if S_h > h or S_l < -h:
            change_points.append(time_index.iloc[i]) # Store datetime
            S_h = 0 # Reset S_h
            S_l = 0 # Reset S_l

    return time_index, cusum_positive, cusum_negative, change_points, h, series_to_analyze




# daily cusum plots for all symbols

all_symbols = ['6E.c.0', 'BZ.c.0', 'CL.c.0', 'TN.c.0', 'UB.c.0', 'ZB.c.0', 'ZF.c.0', 'ZN.c.0', 'ZT.c.0']

# Store all CUSUM dates for both features
all_cusum_dates = {}

features_to_analyze_list = ['rv_next_60'] # Changed to include rv_next_300

for feature_to_analyze in features_to_analyze_list:
    print(f"\n=====Generating Daily CUSUM plots for: {feature_to_analyze}=====")

    fig, axes = plt.subplots(3, 3, figsize=(20, 18), sharex=False, sharey=False)
    fig.suptitle(f'Daily CUSUM Analysis for {feature_to_analyze}', fontsize=20, y = 1)
    fig.subplots_adjust(top=0.80)
    axes = axes.flatten()

    for i, symbol in enumerate(all_symbols):
        ax = axes[i]

        # Run daily CUSUM analysis
        time_index, cusum_positive, cusum_negative, change_points, h, original_series = cusum_daily(sdf_clean, symbol, feature_to_analyze)

        if len(time_index) > 0:

            ax.plot(time_index, cusum_positive, 'g-', label='CUSUM+ (Upward Shift)')
            ax.plot(time_index, cusum_negative, 'r-', label='CUSUM- (Downward Shift)')

            max_cusum_pos_date = None
            min_cusum_neg_date = None
            if len(cusum_positive) > 0 and len(cusum_negative) > 0:
                max_cusum_pos_idx = np.argmax(cusum_positive)
                max_cusum_pos_date = time_index.iloc[max_cusum_pos_idx].strftime("%Y-%m-%d")
                ax.axvline(x=time_index.iloc[max_cusum_pos_idx], color='purple', linestyle='-', linewidth=1.5, label=f'Max CUSUM+ ({max_cusum_pos_date})')

                min_cusum_neg_idx = np.argmin(cusum_negative)
                min_cusum_neg_date = time_index.iloc[min_cusum_neg_idx].strftime("%Y-%m-%d")
                ax.axvline(x=time_index.iloc[min_cusum_neg_idx], color='orange', linestyle='-', linewidth=1.5, label=f'Min CUSUM- ({min_cusum_neg_date})')

            # Store dates
            if symbol not in all_cusum_dates:
                all_cusum_dates[symbol] = {}
            all_cusum_dates[symbol][f'{feature_to_analyze}_max_cusum_date'] = max_cusum_pos_date
            all_cusum_dates[symbol][f'{feature_to_analyze}_min_cusum_date'] = min_cusum_neg_date

            ax.set_title(f'{symbol}')
            ax.set_ylabel('CUSUM Value')
            ax.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.6)
            ax.legend(loc='upper left', fontsize='small')
            ax.tick_params(axis='x', labelrotation=45)

    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

# helper function

def cusum_intraday(spark_df, symbol_to_analyze, target_date_str, resample_freq, sample_fraction=1.0, feature_col='rv_next_60'):
    """
    Performs CUSUM analysis for intraday.

    Args:
        spark_df (pyspark.sql.DataFrame): The input Spark DataFrame (sdf_clean).
        symbol_to_analyze (str): The financial symbol to analyze.
        target_date_str (str): The specific date to filter for (e.g., 'YYYY-MM-DD').
        resample_freq (str): The resampling frequency (e.g., '5s', '1min', 'H', 'D').
        sample_fraction (float): Fraction of data to sample before converting to Pandas. Defaults to 1.0 to get all data for a specific day.
        feature_col (str): The column to perform CUSUM analysis on.

    Returns:
        tuple: (time_index, cusum_positive, cusum_negative, change_points, h_threshold, series_to_analyze)
    """
    single_symbol_sdf_cusum = (
        spark_df.filter(F.col('symbol') == symbol_to_analyze)
                .filter(F.to_date(F.col('second_bucket')) == F.to_date(F.lit(target_date_str)))
                .select(F.col('second_bucket').cast('string').alias('second_bucket_str'), feature_col)
                .orderBy('second_bucket_str')
    )

    single_symbol_sdf_cusum = single_symbol_sdf_cusum.sample(False, fraction=sample_fraction, seed=42)

    single_symbol_pd_cusum = single_symbol_sdf_cusum.toPandas()

    if single_symbol_pd_cusum.empty:
        return pd.Series(), np.array([]), np.array([]), [], 0.0, np.array([])

    single_symbol_pd_cusum['second_bucket'] = pd.to_datetime(single_symbol_pd_cusum['second_bucket_str'])
    single_symbol_pd_cusum = single_symbol_pd_cusum.drop(columns=['second_bucket_str'])

    single_symbol_pd_cusum_filtered = single_symbol_pd_cusum.dropna(subset=[feature_col])

    # Resample to custom frequency
    resampled_df = (
        single_symbol_pd_cusum_filtered.set_index('second_bucket')
                                         .resample(resample_freq)[feature_col].sum()
                                         .reset_index()
    )
    resampled_df = resampled_df.dropna(subset=[feature_col])

    series_to_analyze = resampled_df[feature_col].values
    time_index = resampled_df['second_bucket']

    if len(series_to_analyze) == 0:
        return time_index, np.array([]), np.array([]), [], 0.0, np.array([])

    mean_series = np.mean(series_to_analyze)
    std_series = np.std(series_to_analyze)

    k = 0.5 * std_series # adjustable slack
    h = 4 * std_series   # adjustable threshold

    cusum_positive = np.zeros_like(series_to_analyze, dtype=float)
    cusum_negative = np.zeros_like(series_to_analyze, dtype=float)

    S_h = 0  # for upward shift detection
    S_l = 0  # for downward shift detection
    change_points = []

    for i, x in enumerate(series_to_analyze):
        S_h = max(0, S_h + (x - mean_series - k))
        S_l = min(0, S_l + (x - mean_series + k))
        cusum_positive[i] = S_h
        cusum_negative[i] = S_l
        if S_h > h or S_l < -h:
            change_points.append(time_index.iloc[i]) # Store datetime
            S_h = 0 # Reset S_h
            S_l = 0 # Reset S_l


    return time_index, cusum_positive, cusum_negative, change_points, h, series_to_analyze




# ploting intraday 'rv_60_next' (resampled in 5s) change points for all the futures

all_symbols = ['6E.c.0', 'BZ.c.0', 'CL.c.0', 'TN.c.0', 'UB.c.0', 'ZB.c.0', 'ZF.c.0', 'ZN.c.0', 'ZT.c.0']
resample_interval = '5s' # Using 5-second resampling

col_name = 'rv_next_60_max_cusum_date'
target_date = '2025-11-21'

if target_date is None:
    print(f"\nSkipping intraday CUSUM for {col_name} as no frequent date was identified.")
else:
    feature_to_analyze = '_'.join(col_name.split('_')[:3])


    print(f"\nIntraday CUSUM Analysis for {feature_to_analyze} on {target_date} (Resampling: {resample_interval})")

    fig, axes = plt.subplots(3, 3, figsize=(20, 18), sharex=False, sharey=False)

    fig.suptitle(f'Intraday CUSUM for {feature_to_analyze} on {target_date}', fontsize=20, y=1)
    axes = axes.flatten()

    intraday_times_per_symbol = {} # To store times

    for i, symbol in enumerate(all_symbols):
        ax = axes[i]

        # Run CUSUM analysis
        time_index, cusum_positive, cusum_negative, change_points, h, original_series = cusum_intraday(
            sdf_clean, symbol, target_date, resample_interval, sample_fraction=1.0, feature_col=feature_to_analyze
        )

        max_cusum_pos_time_str = None
        min_cusum_neg_time_str = None

        if len(time_index) > 0:

            ax.plot(time_index, cusum_positive, color='blue', linestyle='-', label='CUSUM+ (Upward Shift)',alpha=0.6)
            ax.plot(time_index, cusum_negative, color='orange', linestyle='-', label='CUSUM- (Downward Shift)',alpha=0.6)


            ax.axhline(y=h, color='green', linestyle='-.', label='Upper Threshold',alpha=0.8)
            ax.axhline(y=-h, color='red', linestyle='-.', label='Lower Threshold',alpha=0.8)


            if len(cusum_positive) > 0 and len(cusum_negative) > 0:
                max_cusum_pos_idx = np.argmax(cusum_positive)
                max_cusum_pos_time_str = time_index.iloc[max_cusum_pos_idx].strftime("%H:%M:%S")
                ax.axvline(x=time_index.iloc[max_cusum_pos_idx], color='purple', linestyle='-', linewidth=1.5, label=f'Max CUSUM+ ({max_cusum_pos_time_str})')


                min_cusum_neg_idx = np.argmin(cusum_negative)
                min_cusum_neg_time_str = time_index.iloc[min_cusum_neg_idx].strftime("%H:%M:%S")
                ax.axvline(x=time_index.iloc[min_cusum_neg_idx], color='orange', linestyle='-', linewidth=1.5, label=f'Min CUSUM- ({min_cusum_neg_time_str})')

            ax.set_title(f'{symbol}')
            ax.set_ylabel('CUSUM Value')
            ax.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.6)
            ax.legend(loc='upper left', fontsize='small')
            ax.tick_params(axis='x', labelrotation=45)

        intraday_times_per_symbol[symbol] = {
            'Max_CUSUM_Time': max_cusum_pos_time_str,
            'Min_CUSUM_Time': min_cusum_neg_time_str
        }

    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()





intraday_df = pd.DataFrame.from_dict(intraday_times_per_symbol, orient='index')
intraday_df.index.name = 'Symbol'

print(f"\nIntraday CUSUM Max/Min Times for {feature_to_analyze} on {target_date}:")
print(intraday_df.to_string())

records.content:
Construct the page matrix, P

Compute the SVD of page matrix,  P = \sum_{i = 1}^r \sigma_i u_i v_i^\top

Truncate the SVD by thresholding and renormalize, Q = \frac{1}{\widehat{\rho}} \sum_{i = 1}^{\tilde{r}} \sigma_i u_i v_i^\top

Reconstruct the time series from Q.

hierarchy.lvl1: SSA

records.content:
Page Matrix takes a time series y(1), \dots, y(T) and shapes it into a matrix:P(y, L) = \begin{bmatrix}
y(1) & y(L + 1) & \dots & y(T - L + 1) \\
        y(2) & y(L + 2) & \dots & y(T - L + 2) \\
        \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
        y(L) & y(2L) & \dots & y(T) \\
\end{bmatrix}

The main reason for Page Matrix instead of Hankel Matrix is the computation complexity.

records.content:
# Define the window length for SSA
L = 60

def page_matrix(df, L):
    """
    Creates a 'page matrix' (trajectory matrix) from a multi-variate time series DataFrame
    using a non-overlapping window.

    Args:
        df (pd.DataFrame): Input DataFrame where rows are time steps and columns are series.
                           Index should be datetime or sorted.
        L (int): Window length (number of time steps per window).

    Returns:
        tuple: (np.ndarray page_matrix, int actual_timesteps_used)
    """
    N = df.shape[0] # Total number of time steps
    K = df.shape[1]


    num_windows = N // L

    result_page_matrix = np.zeros((L, num_windows * K)) # Renamed local variable

    actual_timesteps_used = num_windows * L

    for k in range(K): #
        series_data = df.iloc[:actual_timesteps_used, k].values
        for j in range(num_windows):
            start_idx = j * L
            end_idx = start_idx + L
            result_page_matrix[:, j * K + k] = series_data[start_idx:end_idx] # Updated variable usage

    return result_page_matrix, actual_timesteps_used # Updated variable usage



def reconstruct_series(reconstructed_page_matrix, L, n_features, original_index, original_columns):
    """
    Reconstructs the original time series from the reconstructed page matrix.

    Args:
        reconstructed_page_matrix (np.ndarray): The matrix reconstructed from SVD components.
        L (int): Window length.
        n_features (int): Number of original series (columns in original DataFrame).
        original_index (pd.Index): The datetime index of the original DataFrame.
        original_columns (list): List of column names from the original DataFrame.

    Returns:
        pd.DataFrame: Reconstructed time series DataFrame.
    """
    if reconstructed_page_matrix.size == 0:
        return pd.DataFrame()

    num_rows_matrix, num_cols_matrix = reconstructed_page_matrix.shape
    num_windows = num_cols_matrix // n_features # Total number of "column" windows across all series

    reconstructed_length_per_series = num_windows * L

    reconstructed_data = {col: np.zeros(reconstructed_length_per_series) for col in original_columns}
    reconstructed_counts = {col: np.zeros(reconstructed_length_per_series) for col in original_columns}

    for feature_idx, col_name in enumerate(original_columns):
        series_matrix_cols = range(feature_idx, num_cols_matrix, n_features) # Columns in page_matrix corresponding to this series

        for window_idx, col_in_matrix in enumerate(series_matrix_cols):
            start_in_full_series = window_idx * L
            end_in_full_series = start_in_full_series + L

            reconstructed_data[col_name][start_in_full_series:end_in_full_series] += reconstructed_page_matrix[:, col_in_matrix]
            reconstructed_counts[col_name][start_in_full_series:end_in_full_series] += 1

    for col_name in original_columns:
        reconstructed_data[col_name] = np.divide(reconstructed_data[col_name], reconstructed_counts[col_name],
                                                out=np.zeros_like(reconstructed_data[col_name]),
                                                where=reconstructed_counts[col_name]!=0)

    reconstructed_df = pd.DataFrame(reconstructed_data, columns=original_columns)

    reconstructed_df.index = original_index[:reconstructed_length_per_series]

    return reconstructed_df



# evaluation helper

from sklearn.metrics import r2_score

def calculate_rmse(original, reconstructed):
  return np.sqrt(np.mean((original - reconstructed)**2))

# Retrieve group_top_features
groups = {
    'EURO FX': ['6E.c.0'],
    'Crude Oil': ['CL.c.0', 'BZ.c.0'],
    'US Treasury': ['ZT.c.0','ZF.c.0','ZN.c.0','ZB.c.0','TN.c.0','UB.c.0']
}

# Principal components matrix
components_matrix = pca_model.pc.toArray()

def get_vector_element(vector, index):
    if vector is None:
        return None
    return float(vector[index])

get_vector_element_udf = udf(get_vector_element, DoubleType())

group_top_features = {}

print("Top 5 Contributing Features for each group...")
for group_name, symbols in groups.items():

    group_df = train_pca.filter(F.col('symbol').isin(symbols))

    pca_component_cols = [f"pc_{i}" for i in range(len(FEATURE_COLS))]
    for i in range(len(FEATURE_COLS)):
        group_df = group_df.withColumn(f"pc_{i}", get_vector_element_udf(F.col("pca_features"), F.lit(i)))

    # Calculate the mean of each PCA component for the group
    avg_pca_scores_row = group_df.select([F.avg(c).alias(c) for c in pca_component_cols]).first()

    if avg_pca_scores_row:
        avg_pca_scores = np.array([avg_pca_scores_row[c] for c in pca_component_cols])
    else:
        avg_pca_scores = np.zeros(len(FEATURE_COLS))

    # Project the average PCA scores back to the original feature space
    feature_contributions = np.dot(components_matrix, avg_pca_scores)

    feature_contributions_series = pd.Series(feature_contributions, index=FEATURE_COLS)

    top_5_features = feature_contributions_series.abs().sort_values(ascending=False).head(5).index.tolist()
    group_top_features[group_name] = top_5_features
    print(f"  {group_name}: {top_5_features}")

print("Top features complete.")



# ---Construct Page Matrix and Truncate SVD on Training Data ---

FORECAST_HORIZON = 60

U_store = {}
Sigma_store = {}
Vt_store = {}
n_components_to_use_store = {}

original_training_series = {}
reconstructed_training_series = {}

print("--- SSA: SVD Decomposition (Training Data) ---")

for group_name, symbols in groups.items():
    print(f"\nProcessing training data for group: {group_name}")

    top_features = group_top_features.get(group_name, [])
    if not top_features:
        print(f"  No top features defined for {group_name}.")
        continue

    # prepare the training data
    features_for_mssa = ['second_bucket', 'symbol', 'rv_next_60', 'rv_next_300'] + top_features
    mssa_input_spark_df = train_df.filter(F.col('symbol').isin(symbols)).select(features_for_mssa)
    sample_fraction_for_pandas = 1.0 #adjust for memory limit
    mssa_input_pd_df = mssa_input_spark_df.sample(False, sample_fraction_for_pandas, seed=42).toPandas()

    if mssa_input_pd_df.empty:
        print(f"  No data after sampling for {group_name}. Skipping.")
        continue

    # sort mssa_input_pd_df, melt, and pivot
    mssa_input_pd_df = mssa_input_pd_df.sort_values(by=['second_bucket', 'symbol'])
    mssa_melted_df = mssa_input_pd_df.melt(id_vars=['second_bucket', 'symbol'], var_name='feature', value_name='value')
    mssa_melted_df['series_name'] = mssa_melted_df['feature'] + '_' + mssa_melted_df['symbol']
    original_df_for_reconstruction = mssa_melted_df.pivot_table(index='second_bucket', columns='series_name', values='value')
    original_df_for_reconstruction = original_df_for_reconstruction.ffill().bfill()

    # handle insufficient data
    if original_df_for_reconstruction.empty or len(original_df_for_reconstruction) < L:
        print(f"  Insufficient original data points for reconstruction for {group_name} (need at least {L} timesteps). Skipping.")
        continue

    print(f"  Preparing training data for {group_name}. Original shape: {original_df_for_reconstruction.shape}")

    # construct page matrix
    current_page_matrix, actual_timesteps_used = page_matrix(original_df_for_reconstruction, L)

    if current_page_matrix.size == 0:
        print(f"  Failed to create page matrix for {group_name}. Skipping.")
        continue

    print(f"  Page matrix created with shape: {current_page_matrix.shape}")

    # perform SVD
    U, Sigma, Vt = np.linalg.svd(current_page_matrix, full_matrices=False)

    # store results

    U_store[group_name] = U
    Sigma_store[group_name] = Sigma
    Vt_store[group_name] = Vt

    # determine the number of components for reconstruction (explained variance > 90%)
    explained_variance_ratio = (Sigma ** 2) / np.sum(Sigma ** 2)
    cumulative_explained_variance = np.cumsum(explained_variance_ratio)
    n_components_to_use = np.where(cumulative_explained_variance >= 0.90)[0][0] + 1
    n_components_to_use_store[group_name] = n_components_to_use

    # print(f"  Number of components explaining >90% variance for {group_name}: {n_components_to_use}")

    # reconstruct the series by summing elementary matrices
    reconstructed_page_matrix = np.zeros_like(current_page_matrix, dtype=float)
    for i in range(n_components_to_use):
        elementary_matrix = Sigma[i] * np.outer(U[:, i], Vt[i, :])
        reconstructed_page_matrix += elementary_matrix

    # use reconstruct_series to get the final reconstructed_df
    n_features = original_df_for_reconstruction.shape[1]
    reconstructed_df = reconstruct_series(
        reconstructed_page_matrix,
        L,
        n_features,
        original_df_for_reconstruction.index,
        original_df_for_reconstruction.columns.tolist()
    )


    original_training_series[group_name] = original_df_for_reconstruction.iloc[:actual_timesteps_used]
    reconstructed_training_series[group_name] = reconstructed_df

    print(f"  Reconstruction for {group_name} training data complete. Reconstructed series shape: {reconstructed_df.shape}")

print("\n--- SSA: SVD Decomposition and Reconstruction (Training Data) Complete ---")

# --- Reconstruct Validation Dataset ---

original_validation_series = {}
reconstructed_validation_series = {}

print("\n--- SSA: Reconstruction (Validation Data) ---")

for group_name, symbols in groups.items():
    print(f"\nProcessing validation data for group: {group_name}")

    top_features = group_top_features.get(group_name, [])
    if not top_features:
        print(f"  No top features defined for {group_name}. Skipping.")
        continue

    # prepare the validation data
    features_for_mssa = ['second_bucket', 'symbol', 'rv_next_60', 'rv_next_300'] + top_features
    mssa_input_spark_df_val = val_df.filter(F.col('symbol').isin(symbols)).select(features_for_mssa)
    sample_fraction_for_pandas = 1.0  #adjust for memory limit
    mssa_input_pd_df_val = mssa_input_spark_df_val.sample(False, sample_fraction_for_pandas, seed=42).toPandas()

    if mssa_input_pd_df_val.empty:
        print(f"  No data after sampling for {group_name} in validation. Skipping.")
        continue

    # sort mssa_input_pd_df_val, melt, and pivot
    mssa_input_pd_df_val = mssa_input_pd_df_val.sort_values(by=['second_bucket', 'symbol'])
    mssa_melted_df_val = mssa_input_pd_df_val.melt(id_vars=['second_bucket', 'symbol'], var_name='feature', value_name='value')
    mssa_melted_df_val['series_name'] = mssa_melted_df_val['feature'] + '_' + mssa_melted_df_val['symbol']
    original_df_for_reconstruction_val = mssa_melted_df_val.pivot_table(index='second_bucket', columns='series_name', values='value')
    original_df_for_reconstruction_val = original_df_for_reconstruction_val.ffill().bfill()

    # handle insufficient data
    if original_df_for_reconstruction_val.empty or len(original_df_for_reconstruction_val) < L:
        print(f"  Insufficient original data points for reconstruction for {group_name} in validation (need at least {L} timesteps).")
        continue

    print(f"  Preparing validation data for {group_name}. Original shape: {original_df_for_reconstruction_val.shape}")

    # construct page matrix for validation data
    current_page_matrix_val, actual_timesteps_used_val = page_matrix(original_df_for_reconstruction_val, L)

    if current_page_matrix_val.size == 0:
        print(f"  Failed to create page matrix for {group_name} in validation data.")
        continue

    print(f"  Validation page matrix created with shape: {current_page_matrix_val.shape}")

    # use singular vectors U from the training data for reconstruction

    if group_name not in U_store or group_name not in n_components_to_use_store:
        print(f"  Training SSA components not found for {group_name}. Skipping validation reconstruction.")
        continue

    U_train_group = U_store[group_name]
    n_components_to_use_group = n_components_to_use_store[group_name]

    # Extract the relevant U components from training

    if U_train_group.shape[1] < n_components_to_use_group:
        print(f"  Warning: Not enough components in U_train for {group_name} ({U_train_group.shape[1]} < {n_components_to_use_group}). Using all available components for reconstruction.")
        U_train_r = U_train_group
    else:
        U_train_r = U_train_group[:, :n_components_to_use_group]

    # Check for dimension compatibility before projection
    if U_train_r.shape[0] != current_page_matrix_val.shape[0]:
        print(f"  Warning: Dimension mismatch for {group_name}. U_train_r rows ({U_train_r.shape[0]}) != current_page_matrix_val rows ({current_page_matrix_val.shape[0]}). Skipping reconstruction for this group.")
        reconstructed_validation_series[group_name] = pd.DataFrame()
        continue

    # apply the trained U matrix to the validation data for reconstruction
    reconstructed_page_matrix_val = U_train_r @ U_train_r.T @ current_page_matrix_val


    n_features_val = original_df_for_reconstruction_val.shape[1]
    reconstructed_df_val = reconstruct_series(
        reconstructed_page_matrix_val,
        L,
        n_features_val,
        original_df_for_reconstruction_val.index,
        original_df_for_reconstruction_val.columns.tolist()
    )


    original_validation_series[group_name] = original_df_for_reconstruction_val.iloc[:actual_timesteps_used_val]
    reconstructed_validation_series[group_name] = reconstructed_df_val

    print(f"  Reconstruction for {group_name} validation data complete. Reconstructed series shape: {reconstructed_df_val.shape}")

print("\n--- SSA Reconstruction (Validation Dataset) Summary ---")
for group_name in groups:
    if group_name in reconstructed_validation_series:
        print(f"Group '{group_name}': Original validation series shape: {original_validation_series[group_name].shape}, Reconstructed validation series shape: {reconstructed_validation_series[group_name].shape}")
    else:
        print(f"Group '{group_name}': Validation reconstruction skipped or failed.")



# --- Visualization of Validation Data Reconstruction ---
print("\n--- Visualizing Validation Data Reconstruction ( Top Features and Targets )---")

for group_name in groups:
    if group_name not in original_validation_series or original_validation_series[group_name].empty:
        print(f"Skipping visualization for '{group_name}' validation data: No original validation series data.")
        continue

    original_df_val = original_validation_series[group_name]
    reconstructed_df_val = reconstructed_validation_series.get(group_name, pd.DataFrame())

    if reconstructed_df_val.empty:
        print(f"Skipping visualization for '{group_name}' validation data: No reconstructed validation series data.")
        continue

    print(f"\nGroup (Validation Data): {group_name}")

    series_to_plot_val = original_df_val.columns[:min(5, len(original_df_val.columns))]

    num_plots = len(series_to_plot_val)
    if num_plots == 0:
        print(f"No series to plot for group {group_name}.")
        continue

    fig, axes = plt.subplots(num_plots, 1, figsize=(15, 6 * num_plots), sharex=False)
    if num_plots == 1:
        axes = [axes]

    for i, series_name in enumerate(series_to_plot_val):
        original_series_val = original_df_val[series_name]
        reconstructed_series_val = reconstructed_df_val[series_name]

        # Plotting
        axes[i].plot(original_series_val.index, original_series_val.values, label='Original Validation', alpha=0.7)
        axes[i].plot(reconstructed_series_val.index, reconstructed_series_val.values, label='Reconstructed Validation', linestyle='--')

        axes[i].set_title(f'Validation Data: {series_name} - Original vs. Reconstructed')
        axes[i].set_ylabel('Value')
        axes[i].legend()
        axes[i].grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.6)
        axes[i].tick_params(axis='x', rotation=45)

    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

print("\n--- Validation Data Visualization Complete ---")
