# --- Perform SSA Forecast on Test Data ---

forecasted_series_test = {}
original_test_series = {}

print("\n--- Performing SSA Forecast on Test Data (Top Features and Targets) ---")

for group_name, symbols in groups.items():
    print(f"\nProcessing test data for group: {group_name}")

    if group_name not in U_store or group_name not in n_components_to_use_store:
        print(f"  SSA model components not found for {group_name}. ")
        continue

    top_features = group_top_features.get(group_name, [])
    if not top_features:
        print(f"  No top features defined for {group_name}. ")
        continue

    # prepare the test data
    features_for_mssa = ['second_bucket', 'symbol', 'rv_next_60', 'rv_next_300'] + top_features
    mssa_input_spark_df_test = test_df.filter(F.col('symbol').isin(symbols)).select(features_for_mssa)
    sample_fraction_for_pandas = 1.0 #adjust for memory limit

    mssa_input_pd_df_test = mssa_input_spark_df_test.sample(False, sample_fraction_for_pandas, seed=42).toPandas()

    if mssa_input_pd_df_test.empty:
        print(f"  No data after sampling for {group_name}. Skipping.")
        continue

    # sort mssa_input_pd_df_test, melt, and pivot
    mssa_input_pd_df_test = mssa_input_pd_df_test.sort_values(by=['second_bucket', 'symbol'])
    mssa_melted_df_test = mssa_input_pd_df_test.melt(id_vars=['second_bucket', 'symbol'], var_name='feature', value_name='value')
    mssa_melted_df_test['series_name'] = mssa_melted_df_test['feature'] + '_' + mssa_melted_df_test['symbol']
    original_df_for_forecast_test = mssa_melted_df_test.pivot_table(index='second_bucket', columns='series_name', values='value')
    original_df_for_forecast_test = original_df_for_forecast_test.ffill().bfill()

    # handle insufficient data
    if original_df_for_forecast_test.empty or len(original_df_for_forecast_test) < L:
        print(f"  Insufficient original data points for forecasting for {group_name} in test data (need at least {L} timesteps).")
        continue

    print(f"  Preparing test data for {group_name}. Original shape: {original_df_for_forecast_test.shape}")

    # construct the page matrix for test data
    page_matrix_test, actual_timesteps_used_test = page_matrix(original_df_for_forecast_test, L)

    if page_matrix_test.size == 0:
        print(f"  Failed to create page matrix for {group_name} in test data.")
        continue

    print(f"  Test page matrix created with shape: {page_matrix_test.shape}")

    # forecast the test series using the SSA components learned from training data

    if group_name not in U_store or group_name not in n_components_to_use_store:
        print(f"  Training SSA components not found for {group_name}. Skipping test forecasting.")
        forecasted_series_test[group_name] = pd.DataFrame()
        continue

    U_train_group = U_store[group_name]
    n_components_to_use_group = n_components_to_use_store[group_name]

    # extract the relevant U components from training
    if U_train_group.shape[1] < n_components_to_use_group:
        print(f"  Warning: Not enough components in U_train for {group_name} ({U_train_group.shape[1]} < {n_components_to_use_group}). Using all available components for forecasting.")
        U_train_r = U_train_group
    else:
        U_train_r = U_train_group[:, :n_components_to_use_group]

    # check for dimension compatibility before projection
    if U_train_r.shape[0] != page_matrix_test.shape[0]:
        print(f"  Warning: Dimension mismatch for {group_name}. U_train_r rows ({U_train_r.shape[0]}) != page_matrix_test rows ({page_matrix_test.shape[0]}). Skipping forecasting for this group.")
        forecasted_series_test[group_name] = pd.DataFrame()
        continue

    # apply the trained U matrix to the test data forecasting

    forecasted_page_matrix_test = U_train_r @ U_train_r.T @ page_matrix_test

    n_features_test = original_df_for_forecast_test.shape[1]
    forecasted_df_current_group = reconstruct_series(
        forecasted_page_matrix_test,
        L,
        n_features_test,
        original_df_for_forecast_test.index,
        original_df_for_forecast_test.columns.tolist()
    )

    original_test_series[group_name] = original_df_for_forecast_test.iloc[:actual_timesteps_used_test]

    forecasted_series_test[group_name] = forecasted_df_current_group
    print(f"  Forecast for {group_name} test data complete. Forecasted series shape: {forecasted_series_test[group_name].shape}")

print("\n--- SSA Forecast on Test Data Complete ---")



# --- Visualization of Test Data Forecast ---

print("\n--- Visualizing Test Data Forecast (Top Features and Targets ) ---")

all_group_test_metrics = []

for group_name in groups:
    if group_name not in original_test_series or original_test_series[group_name].empty:
        print(f"Skipping visualization and evaluation for '{group_name}' test data: No original test series data.")
        continue

    original_df_test = original_test_series[group_name]
    forecast_df_test = forecasted_series_test.get(group_name, pd.DataFrame())

    if forecast_df_test.empty:
        print(f"Skipping visualization and evaluation for '{group_name}' test data: No forecast data.")
        continue

    print(f"\nGroup (Test Data): {group_name}")

    # Metrics calculation for the forecast
    group_rmse_values_test = []
    group_normalized_rmse_values_test = []
    group_r2_values_test = []

    series_to_plot_test = original_df_test.columns[:min(5, len(original_df_test.columns))]

    num_plots = len(series_to_plot_test)
    fig, axes = plt.subplots(num_plots, 1, figsize=(15, 6 * num_plots), sharex=False)
    if num_plots == 1:
        axes = [axes]

    for i, series_name in enumerate(series_to_plot_test):
        original_series_test = original_df_test[series_name]
        forecast_series_test = forecast_df_test[series_name] if series_name in forecast_df_test.columns else pd.Series(dtype=float)

        # Plotting
        axes[i].plot(original_series_test.index, original_series_test.values, label='Original Test', alpha=0.7)
        if not forecast_series_test.empty:
            axes[i].plot(forecast_series_test.index, forecast_series_test.values, label=f'Forecast', linestyle='--')

        axes[i].set_title(f'Test Data: {series_name} - Original & Forecast')
        axes[i].set_ylabel('Value')
        axes[i].legend()
        axes[i].grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.6)
        axes[i].tick_params(axis='x', rotation=45)

        # Calculate metrics
        if not original_series_test.empty and not forecast_series_test.empty:

            aligned_orig, aligned_fore = original_series_test.align(forecast_series_test, join='inner')
            if not aligned_orig.empty:
                rmse = calculate_rmse(aligned_orig, aligned_fore)
                std_orig = aligned_orig.std()
                normalized_rmse = rmse / std_orig if std_orig != 0 else np.nan
                r2 = r2_score(aligned_orig, aligned_fore)

                group_rmse_values_test.append(rmse)
                group_normalized_rmse_values_test.append(normalized_rmse)
                group_r2_values_test.append(r2)

    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

    # Aggregate metrics for the group
    group_test_metrics = {
        'Group': group_name,
        'RMSE (Test)': np.mean(group_normalized_rmse_values_test) if group_normalized_rmse_values_test else np.nan,
        'R-squared (Test)': np.mean(group_r2_values_test) if group_r2_values_test else np.nan
    }

    all_group_test_metrics.append(group_test_metrics)

print("\n--- Test Data Visualization Complete ---")

















# Display the test data forecast results by features and targets

metrics_df = pd.DataFrame(all_reconstruction_metrics)

all_top_features_names = set()
for group, features in group_top_features.items():
    all_top_features_names.update(features)

top_features_metrics_df = metrics_df[
    metrics_df['Series'].apply(
        lambda x: any(tf in x for tf in all_top_features_names) and
                  'rv_next_60' not in x and 'rv_next_300' not in x
    )
].copy()

average_top_features_metrics = top_features_metrics_df.groupby('Group')[['Test RMSE', 'Test R2']].mean().round(4)

target_metrics_df = metrics_df[
    metrics_df['Series'].str.contains('rv_next_60|rv_next_300', regex=True)
].copy()


average_target_metrics = target_metrics_df.groupby('Group')[['Test RMSE', 'Test R2']].mean().round(4)


print("1. Each Group's Top Features Forecast (Test Data):")
print(average_top_features_metrics.sort_values(by='Test R2', ascending=True).to_string())
print("\n" + "="*80 + "\n")

print("2. Each Group's Targets Forecast (Test Data):")
print(average_rv_next_metrics.sort_values(by='Test R2', ascending=True).to_string())

SSA

records.content:
The SSA reconstruction (denoise) and forecast for the top features and targets; averaged by group, demonstrates strong reconstruction capabilities for the top features in the Crude Oil and US Treasury groups, while performance for the EURO FX group is more moderate for its selected top features.

EURO FX:

For hour_utc, n_fill_60, n_modify_60, ret_std_60, and rv_past_60, the model shows moderate to good performance. RMSE values are generally higher on the test set compared to the validation set.

Crude Oil:

This group shows strong performance across its top features such as n_events_60, n_modify_60, ret_std_60, rv_past_60, and sum_size_60. Corresponding RMSE values are low.

US Treasury:

The US Treasury group also exhibits robust reconstruction for its top features: n_cancel_60, n_events_60, n_fill_60, n_modify_60, and sum_size_60. High R2 values are consistent across both test and validation sets.

hierarchy.lvl1: Takeaway
Takeaway

records.content:
Clustering Helps

Global / local, linear / non-linear clusterings help understand the similarites among the features.

Features Extraction by Clusters

It might be more efficient and interpretable to extract the features based on the clustering to improve the model performance.

Violent Volatility Expansion

At 60s or even shorter horizon,volatility is essentially a measure of the speed of price discovery within a single minute or less.

SSA Results Differ among Asset classes

SSA works better in Crude Oil and US Treasury than EURO FX. ItŌĆÖs worthwhile to investigate into EURO FX inherent noise or additional exogenous variables.

hierarchy.lvl1: Limitation
Limitation

records.content:
Computation Complexity:

SVD: for a time series of length N and window L, the Page Matrix Embedding generates non-overlapping columns whereas Hankel Matrix Embedding generates the overlapping columns by sliding a window of length of L across the data one step at a time, the resulting computational complexity is O(NL) vs O(NL^2), so Page Matrix embedding was used here.

Lack of parallelism in t-SNE

Matrix Embedding:

Though Page Matrix is more computational efficient, Hankel captures the ŌĆ£flowŌĆØ of the time series, making it better for real-time filtering and smoothing.

Hyperparameter Tuning:

Hierarchical Clustering ( features selected, distance metric )

SVD ( truncate thresholding )

Matrix Embedding ( window size)

This notebook did not focus on the hyperparameter tuning, we leverage on the domain knowledge as a starting point.

hierarchy.lvl1: Application
Application

records.content:
Clustering: both local (t-SNE) and hierarchical clustering could lead to discover the links among the trading instruments in terms of hedging, risk management and portfolio construction etc.

CUSUM statistics might be useful in change points detection in terms of risk and market regime etc.

hierarchy.lvl1: Further Studies
Further Studies

records.content:
Some possible expansions from here:

CUSUM Statistics can be further studied in the queue model, and we can analyze the stablility of the queue to recognize or possibly predict the market regime changes.

Construct the Hankel Matrix instead of	Page Matrix to better study the autocorrelation for modeling.

SSA denoise can be integrated into the modeling in next steps and reiterate to finetune the hyperparameters such as the window size and SVD truncate threshold.

XGBoost Models for Volatility Forecasting

records.content:
This notebook uses the MBO feature table built from raw CME MBO data to test whether recent market activity can help predict future realized volatility for CME futures contracts.

Realized volatility measures how much price actually moved over a given time window. A higher value means the market was more active or unstable. A lower value means the market was calmer.

This notebook focuses on two forecast horizons:

the next 60 seconds

the next 300 seconds

For each horizon, this notebook evaluates two XGBoost tasks:

classification: predict whether future volatility will be a spike or not

regression: predict the actual future volatility level

Overall, this notebook explores whether recent microstructure features contain useful predictive signal for short-horizon volatility forecasting.

records.content:
Persistence model: assume future volatility looks like recent volatility.

EDA showed that recent realized volatility and future realized volatility are strongly correlated.

For each symbol, the volatility spike threshold is defined as the 90th percentile of the future realized volatility values in the training set. A spike is predicted whenever rv_past_60 exceeds that threshold.

Two horizons are tested:

60 seconds: past 60 seconds predicts next 60 seconds

300 seconds: past 60 seconds predicts next 5 minutes

This provides a benchmark for the XGBoost models and helps show whether the MBO microstructure features add predictive signal beyond recent volatility alone.

# track results for each contract
persistence_results = []

# create spark df with only the columns needed for the persistence model
persistence_columns = ["symbol", "session_date", "second_bucket", "rv_past_60", "rv_next_60", "rv_next_300"]
persistence_sdf = sdf.select(*persistence_columns)

# iterate over each symbol
for symbol in symbol_list:

    # filter to one symbol, keeping time order
    symbol_sdf = persistence_sdf.filter(F.col("symbol") == symbol).orderBy("session_date", "second_bucket")

    # convert to pandas df
    symbol_df = symbol_sdf.toPandas()

    # ensure second_bucket is in datetime format
    symbol_df["second_bucket"] = pd.to_datetime(symbol_df["second_bucket"])

    # chronologically split by session date: train (70%), validation (15%), and test (15%)
    dates = sorted(symbol_df["session_date"].unique())
    training_cutoff = int(len(dates) * 0.70)
    validation_cutoff = int(len(dates) * 0.85)

    train_dates = dates[:training_cutoff]
    validation_dates = dates[training_cutoff:validation_cutoff]
    test_dates = dates[validation_cutoff:]

    train_df = symbol_df[symbol_df["session_date"].isin(train_dates)].copy()
    validation_df = symbol_df[symbol_df["session_date"].isin(validation_dates)].copy()
    test_df = symbol_df[symbol_df["session_date"].isin(test_dates)].copy()

    # define volatility spike as the 90th percentile of the future target using only the training set
    vol_spike_threshold_60 = train_df["rv_next_60"].quantile(0.90)
    vol_spike_threshold_300 = train_df["rv_next_300"].quantile(0.90)

    # create test labels: 1 = future volatility spike, 0 = no spike
    test_df["vol_spike_60"] = (test_df["rv_next_60"] > vol_spike_threshold_60).astype(int)
    test_df["vol_spike_300"] = (test_df["rv_next_300"] > vol_spike_threshold_300).astype(int)

    # persistence model:
    # predict a future spike whenever current past volatility is already above the spike threshold
    test_pred_60 = (test_df["rv_past_60"] > vol_spike_threshold_60).astype(int)
    test_pred_300 = (test_df["rv_past_60"] > vol_spike_threshold_300).astype(int)

    # get test labels
    y_test_60 = test_df["vol_spike_60"]
    y_test_300 = test_df["vol_spike_300"]

    # evaluate the 60-second persistence model
    accuracy_60 = accuracy_score(y_test_60, test_pred_60)
    precision_60 = precision_score(y_test_60, test_pred_60)
    recall_60 = recall_score(y_test_60, test_pred_60)
    f1_60 = f1_score(y_test_60, test_pred_60)

    # evaluate the 300-second persistence model
    accuracy_300 = accuracy_score(y_test_300, test_pred_300)
    precision_300 = precision_score(y_test_300, test_pred_300)
    recall_300 = recall_score(y_test_300, test_pred_300)
    f1_300 = f1_score(y_test_300, test_pred_300)

    # save summary for this symbol
    persistence_results.append({
        "symbol": symbol,
        "volatility_spike_threshold_60": vol_spike_threshold_60,
        "test_spike_rate_60": y_test_60.mean(),
        "accuracy_60": accuracy_60,
        "precision_60": precision_60,
        "recall_60": recall_60,
        "f1_60": f1_60,
        "volatility_spike_threshold_300": vol_spike_threshold_300,
        "test_spike_rate_300": y_test_300.mean(),
        "accuracy_300": accuracy_300,
        "precision_300": precision_300,
        "recall_300": recall_300,
        "f1_300": f1_300
    })

    #print("complete:", symbol)

    # free memory before moving to the next symbol
    del symbol_sdf, symbol_df, train_df, validation_df, test_df
    del y_test_60, y_test_300, test_pred_60, test_pred_300
    gc.collect()

# put all results into dataframe for evaluation
persistence_results_df = pd.DataFrame(persistence_results).sort_values("f1_60", ascending=False).reset_index(drop=True)

persistence_results_df

hierarchy.lvl1: XGBoost Models for Volatility Forecasting
XGBoost Models for Volatility Forecasting

records.content:
Train one XGBoost classifier for each symbol.

For each symbol, the target is whether realized volatility over the next 60 seconds exceeds the 90th percentile of the training set.

Features come from the EDA-informed feature set. They include recent realized volatility, rolling 60-second activity counts, imbalance features, return dispersion, and hour of day.

A chronological split is used:

70% train

15% validation

15% test

The validation set is used for tuning:

tree depth is searched over [3, 4, 5]

probability threshold is searched from 0.10 to 0.55 in steps of 0.05

The best model is chosen using validation ROC-AUC. ROC-AUC is useful here because it evaluates how well the model ranks spike vs non-spike cases across all possible thresholds.

The best threshold is chosen using validation F1. F1 is helpful because the spike classes are imbalanced, and it balances precision and recall when turning predicted probabilities into final class labels.

Most model settings stay fixed across symbols:

n_estimators = 800: allows enough boosting rounds for the model to learn useful patterns

learning_rate = 0.05: uses smaller boosting steps, which usually leads to more stable learning

subsample = 0.8: each tree sees only part of the training data, which helps reduce overfitting

colsample_bytree = 0.8: each tree uses only part of the feature set, which also helps reduce overfitting

early_stopping_rounds = 30: stops training when validation performance no longer improves, which helps avoid fitting noise

One model is fit per symbol because EDA showed that volatility levels and behavior differ across contracts.

MLflow logs the settings and final metrics for each symbol.

# add columns needed for filtering, ordering, and label creation to feature_cols
xgb_columns = ["symbol", "session_date", "second_bucket", "rv_next_60"] + feature_cols

# create spark df with selected columns
xgb_sdf_60 = sdf.select(*xgb_columns)

# track results for each symbol
results_60 = []

# store model objects for later evaluation
best_models_by_symbol_60 = {}
test_labels_by_symbol_60 = {}
test_prob_by_symbol_60 = {}
test_pred_by_symbol_60 = {}

# tuning grids, using wide threshold grid
max_depth_options = [3, 4, 5]
threshold_options = [0.10, 0.15, 0.20, 0.25, 0.30, 0.35, 0.40, 0.45, 0.50, 0.55]

# MLflow setup
mlflow.set_experiment("cis5450_xgb_classifier_60")

with mlflow.start_run(run_name="xgb_classifier_60_all_symbols"):

    # log settings
    mlflow.log_param("feature_cols", ", ".join(feature_cols))
    mlflow.log_param("max_depth_options", str(max_depth_options))
    mlflow.log_param("threshold_options", str(threshold_options))
    mlflow.log_param("target", "rv_next_60")

    # iterate over each symbol
    for symbol in symbol_list:

        # filter to one symbol and keep rows in time order
        symbol_sdf = xgb_sdf_60.filter(F.col("symbol") == symbol).orderBy("session_date", "second_bucket")

        # convert to pandas and ensure second_bucket in datetime format
        symbol_df = symbol_sdf.toPandas()
        symbol_df["second_bucket"] = pd.to_datetime(symbol_df["second_bucket"])

        # chronological split by session_date
        dates = sorted(symbol_df["session_date"].unique())
        training_cutoff = int(len(dates) * 0.70)
        validation_cutoff = int(len(dates) * 0.85)

        train_df = symbol_df[symbol_df["session_date"].isin(dates[:training_cutoff])].copy()
        validation_df = symbol_df[symbol_df["session_date"].isin(dates[training_cutoff:validation_cutoff])].copy()
        test_df = symbol_df[symbol_df["session_date"].isin(dates[validation_cutoff:])].copy()

        # define spike threshold from training set only
        vol_spike_threshold = train_df["rv_next_60"].quantile(0.90)

        # create binary labels: 1 = future volatility spike, 0 = no spike
        train_df["vol_spike_60"] = (train_df["rv_next_60"] > vol_spike_threshold).astype(int)
        validation_df["vol_spike_60"] = (validation_df["rv_next_60"] > vol_spike_threshold).astype(int)
        test_df["vol_spike_60"] = (test_df["rv_next_60"] > vol_spike_threshold).astype(int)

        # build X and y
        X_train = train_df[feature_cols]
        X_validation = validation_df[feature_cols]
        X_test = test_df[feature_cols]

        y_train = train_df["vol_spike_60"]
        y_validation = validation_df["vol_spike_60"]
        y_test = test_df["vol_spike_60"]

        # choose best model by validation ROC-AUC
        best_auc = -1
        best_model = None

        # fit model for each depth in depth grid
        for depth in max_depth_options:
            xgb_model = XGBClassifier(
                objective="binary:logistic",
                eval_metric="auc",
                n_estimators=800,
                max_depth=depth,
                learning_rate=0.05,
                subsample=0.8,
                colsample_bytree=0.8,
                early_stopping_rounds=30,
                random_state=42,
                n_jobs=-1
            )
            # use validation set for early stopping
            xgb_model.fit(
                X_train,
                y_train,
                eval_set=[(X_validation, y_validation)],
                verbose=False
            )
            # get predicted probabilities and ROC-AUC for the validation set
            validation_prob = xgb_model.predict_proba(X_validation)[:, 1]
            validation_auc = roc_auc_score(y_validation, validation_prob)

            # keep best model seen so far
            if validation_auc > best_auc:
                best_auc = validation_auc
                best_model = xgb_model

        # choose the probability threshold that gives the best validation F1
        best_threshold = 0.50
        best_f1 = -1

        # get validation probabilities from the best model
        best_validation_prob = best_model.predict_proba(X_validation)[:, 1]

        # try each threshold and calculate validation F1
        for threshold in threshold_options:
            validation_pred = (best_validation_prob >= threshold).astype(int)
            validation_f1 = f1_score(y_validation, validation_pred)

            # keep the threshold if it gives a better F1
            if validation_f1 > best_f1:
                best_f1 = validation_f1
                best_threshold = threshold

        # evaluate best model on test set
        test_prob = best_model.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]
        test_pred = (test_prob >= best_threshold).astype(int)
        accuracy = accuracy_score(y_test, test_pred)
        precision = precision_score(y_test, test_pred)
        recall = recall_score(y_test, test_pred)
        f1 = f1_score(y_test, test_pred)
        roc_auc = roc_auc_score(y_test, test_prob)

        # store model objects for later evaluation
        best_models_by_symbol_60[symbol] = best_model
        test_labels_by_symbol_60[symbol] = y_test.copy()
        test_prob_by_symbol_60[symbol] = test_prob.copy()
        test_pred_by_symbol_60[symbol] = test_pred.copy()

        # log metrics
        mlflow.log_metric(f"{symbol}_best_validation_auc", float(best_auc))
        mlflow.log_metric(f"{symbol}_accuracy", float(accuracy))
        mlflow.log_metric(f"{symbol}_precision", float(precision))
        mlflow.log_metric(f"{symbol}_recall", float(recall))
        mlflow.log_metric(f"{symbol}_f1", float(f1))
        mlflow.log_metric(f"{symbol}_roc_auc", float(roc_auc))

        # save summary row
        results_60.append({
            "symbol": symbol,
            "test_spike_rate": y_test.mean(),
            "accuracy": accuracy,
            "precision": precision,
            "recall": recall,
            "f1": f1,
            "roc_auc": roc_auc,
        })

        #print("complete:", symbol)

        # free memory
        del symbol_sdf, symbol_df, train_df, validation_df, test_df
        del X_train, X_validation, X_test, y_train, y_validation, y_test
        del validation_prob, best_validation_prob, test_prob, test_pred, xgb_model, best_model
        gc.collect()

# results dataframe sorted by F1
XGboost_classifier_results_df_60 = pd.DataFrame(results_60).sort_values("f1", ascending=False).reset_index(drop=True)

# save best symbol for later evaluation

records.content:
The mean test spike rate was about 0.220.

Since about 22% of test rows were spikes, this is an imbalanced classification problem.

Therefore, F1 is the main evaluation metric.

We report precision, recall, F1, accuracy, and ROC-AUC.

Precision is the share of predicted spikes that were real spikes.

Recall is the share of real spikes the model caught.

F1 combines precision and recall, so it is the main comparison metric here.

ROC-AUC shows how well the model ranks spikes above non-spikes across thresholds.

Accuracy is not the main focus because a model can score well by mostly predicting no-spike.

On average, XGBoost had:

slightly lower precision than persistence (0.508 vs 0.511)

clearly higher recall (0.671 vs 0.511)

higher F1 (0.572 vs 0.511)

mean ROC-AUC of about 0.837

mean accuracy of about 0.801

The average F1 improvement was about 0.061 across symbols.

So XGBoost improved the overall precision-recall balance.

The main gain came from higher recall.

Essentially, XGBoost caught more real spikes.

Average precision did not improve.

Findings are consistent with the EDA.

Recent realized volatility looked like the strongest single predictor.

So the persistence baseline was already fairly strong at the 60-second horizon.

That helps explain why the gains from XGBoost were positive but still modest.

Overall takeaway:

XGBoost performed better than persistence on average

the gains were modest but consistent

the main benefit was catching more real spikes

records.content:
Train one XGBoost classifier for each symbol.

For each symbol, the target is whether realized volatility over the next 300 seconds exceeds the 90th percentile of the training set.

Features come from the EDA-informed feature set. They include recent realized volatility, rolling 60-second activity counts, imbalance features, return dispersion, and hour of day.

A chronological split is used:

70% train

15% validation

15% test

The validation set is used for tuning:

tree depth is searched over [3, 4, 5]

probability threshold is searched from 0.10 to 0.55 in steps of 0.05

The best model is chosen using validation ROC-AUC. ROC-AUC is useful here because it measures how well the model separates spike vs non-spike cases across thresholds.

The best threshold is chosen using validation F1. F1 is helpful because the spike classes are imbalanced, and it balances precision and recall when converting predicted probabilities into final class labels.

Most model settings stay fixed across symbols:

n_estimators = 800: allows enough boosting rounds for the model to learn useful patterns

learning_rate = 0.05: uses smaller boosting steps, which usually leads to more stable learning

subsample = 0.8: each tree sees only part of the training data, which helps reduce overfitting

colsample_bytree = 0.8: each tree uses only part of the feature set, which also helps reduce overfitting

early_stopping_rounds = 30: stops training when validation performance no longer improves, which helps avoid fitting noise

One model is fit per symbol because EDA showed that volatility levels and behavior differ across contracts.

MLflow logs the settings and final metrics for each symbol.

# add columns needed for filtering, ordering, and label creation to feature_cols
xgb_columns = ["symbol", "session_date", "second_bucket", "rv_next_300"] + feature_cols

# create spark df with selected columns
xgb_sdf_300 = sdf.select(*xgb_columns)

# track results for each symbol
results_300 = []

# store model objects for later evaluation
best_models_by_symbol_300 = {}
test_labels_by_symbol_300 = {}
test_prob_by_symbol_300 = {}
test_pred_by_symbol_300 = {}

# tuning grids, using wide threshold grid
max_depth_options = [3, 4, 5]
threshold_options = [0.10, 0.15, 0.20, 0.25, 0.30, 0.35, 0.40, 0.45, 0.50, 0.55]

# MLflow setup
mlflow.set_experiment("cis5450_xgb_classifier_300")

with mlflow.start_run(run_name="xgb_classifier_300_all_symbols"):

    # log settings
    mlflow.log_param("feature_cols", ", ".join(feature_cols))
    mlflow.log_param("max_depth_options", str(max_depth_options))
    mlflow.log_param("threshold_options", str(threshold_options))
    mlflow.log_param("target", "rv_next_300")

    # iterate over each symbol
    for symbol in symbol_list:

        # filter to one symbol and keep rows in time order
        symbol_sdf = xgb_sdf_300.filter(F.col("symbol") == symbol).orderBy("session_date", "second_bucket")

        # convert to pandas and ensure second bucket in datetime format
        symbol_df = symbol_sdf.toPandas()
        symbol_df["second_bucket"] = pd.to_datetime(symbol_df["second_bucket"])

        # chronological split by session_date
        dates = sorted(symbol_df["session_date"].unique())
        training_cutoff = int(len(dates) * 0.70)
        validation_cutoff = int(len(dates) * 0.85)

        train_df = symbol_df[symbol_df["session_date"].isin(dates[:training_cutoff])].copy()
        validation_df = symbol_df[symbol_df["session_date"].isin(dates[training_cutoff:validation_cutoff])].copy()
        test_df = symbol_df[symbol_df["session_date"].isin(dates[validation_cutoff:])].copy()

        # define spike threshold from training set
        vol_spike_threshold = train_df["rv_next_300"].quantile(0.90)

        # create binary labels: 1 = future volatility spike, 0 = no spike
        train_df["vol_spike_300"] = (train_df["rv_next_300"] > vol_spike_threshold).astype(int)
        validation_df["vol_spike_300"] = (validation_df["rv_next_300"] > vol_spike_threshold).astype(int)
        test_df["vol_spike_300"] = (test_df["rv_next_300"] > vol_spike_threshold).astype(int)

        # build X and y
        X_train = train_df[feature_cols]
        X_validation = validation_df[feature_cols]
        X_test = test_df[feature_cols]

        y_train = train_df["vol_spike_300"]
        y_validation = validation_df["vol_spike_300"]
        y_test = test_df["vol_spike_300"]

        # choose best model by validation ROC-AUC
        best_auc = -1
        best_model = None

        # fit model for each depth in depth grid
        for depth in max_depth_options:
            xgb_model = XGBClassifier(
                objective="binary:logistic",
                eval_metric="auc",
                n_estimators=800,
                max_depth=depth,
                learning_rate=0.05,
                subsample=0.8,
                colsample_bytree=0.8,
                early_stopping_rounds=30,
                random_state=42,
                n_jobs=-1
            )
            # use validation set for early stopping
            xgb_model.fit(
                X_train,
                y_train,
                eval_set=[(X_validation, y_validation)],
                verbose=False
            )
            # get predicted probabilities and ROC-AUC for the validation set
            validation_prob = xgb_model.predict_proba(X_validation)[:, 1]
            validation_auc = roc_auc_score(y_validation, validation_prob)

            # keep best model so far
            if validation_auc > best_auc:
                best_auc = validation_auc
                best_model = xgb_model

        # choose probability threshold that gives best validation F1
        best_threshold = 0.50
        best_f1 = -1

        # get validation probabilities from the best model
        best_validation_prob = best_model.predict_proba(X_validation)[:, 1]

        # try each threshold and calculate validation F1
        for threshold in threshold_options:
            validation_pred = (best_validation_prob >= threshold).astype(int)
            validation_f1 = f1_score(y_validation, validation_pred)

            # keep the threshold if it gives a better F1
            if validation_f1 > best_f1:
                best_f1 = validation_f1
                best_threshold = threshold

        # evaluate best model on test set
        test_prob = best_model.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]
        test_pred = (test_prob >= best_threshold).astype(int)
        accuracy = accuracy_score(y_test, test_pred)
        precision = precision_score(y_test, test_pred)
        recall = recall_score(y_test, test_pred)
        f1 = f1_score(y_test, test_pred)
        roc_auc = roc_auc_score(y_test, test_prob)

        # store model objects for later evaluation
        best_models_by_symbol_300[symbol] = best_model
        test_labels_by_symbol_300[symbol] = y_test.copy()
        test_prob_by_symbol_300[symbol] = test_prob.copy()
        test_pred_by_symbol_300[symbol] = test_pred.copy()

        # log metrics
        mlflow.log_metric(f"{symbol}_best_validation_auc", float(best_auc))
        mlflow.log_metric(f"{symbol}_accuracy", float(accuracy))
        mlflow.log_metric(f"{symbol}_precision", float(precision))
        mlflow.log_metric(f"{symbol}_recall", float(recall))
        mlflow.log_metric(f"{symbol}_f1", float(f1))
        mlflow.log_metric(f"{symbol}_roc_auc", float(roc_auc))

        # save summary row
        results_300.append({
            "symbol": symbol,
            "test_spike_rate": y_test.mean(),
            "accuracy": accuracy,
            "precision": precision,
            "recall": recall,
            "f1": f1,
            "roc_auc": roc_auc,
        })

        #print("complete:", symbol)

        # free memory
        del symbol_sdf, symbol_df, train_df, validation_df, test_df
        del X_train, X_validation, X_test, y_train, y_validation, y_test
        del validation_prob, best_validation_prob, test_prob, test_pred, xgb_model, best_model
        gc.collect()

# results df sorted by F1
XGboost_classifier_results_df_300 = pd.DataFrame(results_300).sort_values("f1", ascending=False).reset_index(drop=True)

# best symbol for later evaluation
best_symbol_300 = XGboost_classifier_results_df_300.loc[0, "symbol"]

XGboost_classifier_results_df_300

records.content:
The mean test spike rate was about 0.212.

So about 21% of test rows were spikes, meaning this is still an imbalanced classification problem.

On average, XGBoost had:

lower precision than persistence (0.584 vs 0.830)

much higher recall (0.705 vs 0.038)

much higher F1 (0.631 vs 0.072)

mean ROC-AUC of about 0.877

mean accuracy of about 0.837

The average F1 improvement was about 0.559 across symbols.

So XGBoost improved the overall precision-recall balance by a wide margin.

The persistence model became much weaker at the 300-second horizon.

It relied on a single 60-second backward-looking volatility signal to classify a spike 300 seconds ahead.

As a result, persistence predicted spikes much less effectively at this longer horizon.

XGBoost was less limited by that issue.

It combined multiple recent signals instead of relying on one threshold rule.

The broader MBO microstructure feature set seems to transfer better to the longer horizon.

Rolling activity, imbalance, and return-dispersion features appear to add useful signal.

Overall takeaway:

XGBoost performed much better than persistence on average

the gains were much larger than in the 60-second case

the broader microstructure feature set mattered more for the 300-second spike task

hierarchy.lvl2: How could a volatility spike signal be used?

hierarchy.lvl2: How could a volatility spike signal be used?

records.content:
A short-horizon volatility spike classifier is most useful as a caution signal, not a directional trading signal.

The model predicts whether near-term volatility is likely to rise, not whether price will go up or down.

Some realistic use cases are:

execution timing: a trader could slow, pause, or split a large order when a volatility spike is predicted. Large orders can take time to work through the market, and trading during a spike can cause slippage (filling at worse prices as the market moves away). A short pause during predicted turbulence can reduce total trading costs.

market making: a market maker could widen spreads or reduce quote size during predicted spike periods. Market makers earn small profits on many trades by quoting both a buy and a sell price, but a sudden price move can leave those quotes stale and cause losses. Widening spreads ahead of a spike is a simple way to stay protected when the market is expected to move fast.

short-horizon risk management: a risk manager could use the signal as an early warning that near-term volatility may rise. Most risk systems react to volatility that has already happened, so a forward-looking signal gives a small amount of lead time to tighten position limits or review exposures before conditions become more unstable.

These use cases fit the classifier especially well because they depend on a simple decision: whether to become more defensive or not.

The 300-second classifier is probably the most practical version for many of these use cases because it gives a little more reaction time than the 60-second version.

Overall takeaway:

the classifier is most useful as a defensive signal

the signal can be used to avoid a bad trading environment rather than predicting price direction

the 300-second horizon is probably the most practical version for real use

records.content:
Train a XGBoost regressor for each symbol.

For each symbol, the target is rv_next_60, which is the level of realized volatility over the next 60 seconds.

Features come from the EDA-informed feature set. They include recent realized volatility, rolling 60-second activity counts, imbalance features, return dispersion, and hour of day.

A chronological split is used:

70% train

15% validation

15% test

The target is modeled on the log1p scale because future realized volatility is strongly right-skewed. This helps reduce the influence of extreme values. Predictions are then converted back to the raw scale before final evaluation.

The validation set is used for tuning:

tree depth is searched over [3, 4, 5]

The best model is chosen using validation RMSE. RMSE is useful here because it measures the typical size of prediction errors, while giving more weight to larger mistakes. This makes it a good fit for a volatility forecasting task, where large misses matter more.

Most model settings stay fixed across symbols:

n_estimators = 800: allows enough boosting rounds for the model to learn useful patterns

learning_rate = 0.05: uses smaller boosting steps, which usually leads to more stable learning

subsample = 0.8: each tree sees only part of the training data, which helps reduce overfitting

colsample_bytree = 0.8: each tree uses only part of the feature set, which also helps reduce overfitting

early_stopping_rounds = 30: stops training when validation performance no longer improves, which helps avoid fitting noise

A persistence model is also included for comparison. It uses rv_past_60 as a simple forecast of future volatility.

Final results are reported using RMSE, MAE, and R┬▓. MLflow logs the settings and final metrics for each symbol.

# add columns needed for filtering, ordering, and target creation to feature_cols
xgb_columns = ["symbol", "session_date", "second_bucket", "rv_next_60"] + feature_cols

# create spark df with xgb_columns
xgb_sdf_60 = sdf.select(*xgb_columns)

# track results for each symbol
reg_results_60 = []

# store best models and test results by symbol for later evaluation
best_reg_models_by_symbol_60 = {}
reg_test_actual_by_symbol_60 = {}
reg_test_pred_by_symbol_60 = {}

# depth grid
max_depth_options = [3, 4, 5]

# MLflow setup
mlflow.set_experiment("cis5450_xgb_regressor_60")

with mlflow.start_run(run_name="xgb_regressor_60_all_symbols"):

    # log settings
    mlflow.log_param("feature_cols", ", ".join(feature_cols))
    mlflow.log_param("max_depth_options", str(max_depth_options))
    mlflow.log_param("target", "log1p(rv_next_60)")

    # iterate over each symbol and fit an XGBoost regressor
    for symbol in symbol_list:

        # filter to one symbol and keep rows in time order
        symbol_sdf = xgb_sdf_60.filter(F.col("symbol") == symbol).orderBy("session_date", "second_bucket")

        # convert spark df to pandas df for given symbol
        symbol_df = symbol_sdf.toPandas()

        # ensure second_bucket is in datetime format
        symbol_df["second_bucket"] = pd.to_datetime(symbol_df["second_bucket"])

        # EDA showed future realized volatility is heavily right-skewed
        # transform target using log1p
        symbol_df["log_rv_next_60"] = np.log1p(symbol_df["rv_next_60"])

        # split by session date: first 70% of sessions = train, next 15% = validation, last 15% = test
        dates = sorted(symbol_df["session_date"].unique())
        training_cutoff = int(len(dates) * 0.70)
        validation_cutoff = int(len(dates) * 0.85)

        train_df = symbol_df[symbol_df["session_date"].isin(dates[:training_cutoff])].copy()
        validation_df = symbol_df[symbol_df["session_date"].isin(dates[training_cutoff:validation_cutoff])].copy()
        test_df = symbol_df[symbol_df["session_date"].isin(dates[validation_cutoff:])].copy()

        # build X from selected feature columns
        X_train = train_df[feature_cols]
        X_validation = validation_df[feature_cols]
        X_test = test_df[feature_cols]

        # build y from log-transformed target
        y_train = train_df["log_rv_next_60"]
        y_validation = validation_df["log_rv_next_60"]
        y_validation_raw = validation_df["rv_next_60"]
        y_test_raw = test_df["rv_next_60"]

        # persistence model
        # use current 60-second realized vol as forecast for next 60-second realized vol
        persistence_pred = test_df["rv_past_60"].values
        persistence_rmse = np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(y_test_raw, persistence_pred))
        persistence_mae = mean_absolute_error(y_test_raw, persistence_pred)
        persistence_r2 = r2_score(y_test_raw, persistence_pred)

        # select model by validation RMSE on raw scale
        best_val_rmse = float("inf")
        best_depth = None
        best_model = None

        for depth in max_depth_options:

            xgb_model = XGBRegressor(
                objective="reg:squarederror",
                eval_metric="rmse",
                n_estimators=800,
                max_depth=depth,
                learning_rate=0.05,
                subsample=0.8,
                colsample_bytree=0.8,
                early_stopping_rounds=30,
                random_state=42,
                n_jobs=-1
            )
            # fit model using the validation set for early stopping
            xgb_model.fit(
                X_train,
                y_train,
                eval_set=[(X_validation, y_validation)],
                verbose=False
            )

            # calculate validation RMSE on raw scale
            validation_pred = np.maximum(np.expm1(xgb_model.predict(X_validation)), 0)
            val_rmse = np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(y_validation_raw, validation_pred))

            # keep model with the lowest validation RMSE
            if val_rmse < best_val_rmse:
                best_val_rmse = val_rmse
                best_depth = depth
                best_model = xgb_model

        # evaluate the best model on the test set
        # predict on log scale, then convert back to raw scale with expm1
        test_pred = np.maximum(np.expm1(best_model.predict(X_test)), 0)

        xgb_rmse = np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(y_test_raw, test_pred))
        xgb_mae = mean_absolute_error(y_test_raw, test_pred)
        xgb_r2 = r2_score(y_test_raw, test_pred)

        # store best model and test results by symbol for later evaluation
        best_reg_models_by_symbol_60[symbol] = best_model
        reg_test_actual_by_symbol_60[symbol] = y_test_raw.copy()
        reg_test_pred_by_symbol_60[symbol] = test_pred.copy()

        # log key info
        mlflow.log_param(f"{symbol}_best_depth", best_depth)
        mlflow.log_param(f"{symbol}_best_iteration", best_model.best_iteration)
        mlflow.log_metric(f"{symbol}_best_val_rmse", float(best_val_rmse))
        mlflow.log_metric(f"{symbol}_persistence_rmse", float(persistence_rmse))
        mlflow.log_metric(f"{symbol}_persistence_mae", float(persistence_mae))
        mlflow.log_metric(f"{symbol}_persistence_r2", float(persistence_r2))
        mlflow.log_metric(f"{symbol}_xgb_rmse", float(xgb_rmse))
        mlflow.log_metric(f"{symbol}_xgb_mae", float(xgb_mae))
        mlflow.log_metric(f"{symbol}_xgb_r2", float(xgb_r2))

        # store summary for this symbol
        reg_results_60.append({
            "symbol": symbol,
            "persistence_rmse": persistence_rmse,
            "persistence_mae": persistence_mae,
            "persistence_r2": persistence_r2,
            "xgb_rmse": xgb_rmse,
            "xgb_mae": xgb_mae,
            "xgb_r2": xgb_r2,
            "rmse_improvement": persistence_rmse - xgb_rmse,
        })

        #print("complete:", symbol)

        # free memory before moving to the next symbol
        del symbol_sdf, symbol_df, train_df, validation_df, test_df
        del X_train, X_validation, X_test, y_train, y_validation, y_validation_raw, y_test_raw
        del persistence_pred, validation_pred, test_pred, xgb_model, best_model
        gc.collect()

# results df sorted by RMSE
XGboost_regressor_results_df_60 = pd.DataFrame(reg_results_60).sort_values("xgb_rmse", ascending=True).reset_index(drop=True)

# select top symbol for later evaluation
best_reg_symbol_60 = XGboost_regressor_results_df_60.loc[0, "symbol"]

XGboost_regressor_results_df_60





summary_60 = XGboost_regressor_results_df_60[["persistence_rmse", "xgb_rmse", "persistence_mae", "xgb_mae", "persistence_r2", "xgb_r2", "rmse_improvement"]].agg(["mean", "min", "max"])

summary_60

records.content:
Train a XGBoost regressor for each symbol.

For each symbol, the target is rv_next_300, which is the level of realized volatility over the next 300 seconds.

Features come from the EDA-informed feature set. They include recent realized volatility, rolling 60-second activity counts, imbalance features, return dispersion, and hour of day.

A chronological split is used:

70% train

15% validation

15% test

The target is modeled on the log1p scale because future realized volatility is strongly right-skewed. This helps reduce the influence of extreme values. Predictions are then converted back to the raw scale before final evaluation.

The validation set is used for tuning:

tree depth is searched over [3, 4, 5]

The best model is chosen using validation RMSE. RMSE is useful here because it measures the typical size of prediction errors, while giving more weight to larger mistakes. This makes it a good fit for a volatility forecasting task, where large misses matter more.

Most model settings stay fixed across symbols:

n_estimators = 800: allows enough boosting rounds for the model to learn useful patterns

learning_rate = 0.05: uses smaller boosting steps, which usually leads to more stable learning

subsample = 0.8: each tree sees only part of the training data, which helps reduce overfitting

colsample_bytree = 0.8: each tree uses only part of the feature set, which also helps reduce overfitting

early_stopping_rounds = 30: stops training when validation performance no longer improves, which helps avoid fitting noise

records.content:
As with the 60-second regressor, we focus on RMSE.

We also report MAE and R┬▓ as supporting context.

On average, XGBoost improved over the persistence baseline:

mean RMSE fell from about 0.000405 to 0.000264

mean MAE fell from about 0.000287 to 0.000154

mean R┬▓ increased from about -0.190 to 0.523

The average RMSE improvement was about 0.000141 across symbols.

This suggests that the XGBoost regressor usually predicts next-300-second volatility more accurately than the simple persistence forecast.

A likely reason is that the 300-second target depends on more than a single recent volatility signal.

Recent volatility alone appears to be a weaker predictor at this longer horizon.

XGBoost seems better able to use the broader market state by combining rolling activity, trade, imbalance, and return-dispersion features.

Overall takeaway:

XGBoost performed much better than persistence on average

the improvement was clearly larger than in the 60-second case

the broader microstructure feature set appears to add much more value at the longer horizon

records.content:
Across both classification and regression, the results point in the same direction.

Recent volatility was a useful baseline, but the broader MBO microstructure feature set usually improved performance.

At the shorter 60-second horizon, the gains were real but modest.

This suggests that recent realized volatility already contains a lot of near-term signal.

However, the feature set still helped, especially for spike detection.

At the longer 300-second horizon, the gains were much larger.

This suggests that recent volatility alone becomes a weaker predictor as the forecast window gets longer.

Rolling activity, imbalance, trade, and return-dispersion features appear to add much more useful information.

The classifier and regressor results tell a consistent story:

microstructure features helped at the shorter 60-second horizon

microstructure features helped more clearly at the longer 300-second horizon

Overall takeaway:

recent market activity did help predict future realized volatility

the predictive signal was strongest at the 300-second horizon

recent microstructure features contained useful predictive signal beyond recent volatility alone

records.content:
Model performance was strongest for the Treasury contracts, especially ZF.c.0, ZN.c.0, and ZT.c.0.

Performance was weaker for some of the less liquid contracts, especially 6E.c.0.

This does not appear to be a sample-size issue, but rather fits the EDA story.

The Treasury contracts showed denser and more consistent market activity, so the rolling microstructure features likely contained stronger signal.

In contrast, contracts with sparser or less stable order flow likely produced noisier activity features.

The 6E.c.0 results were especially weak.

One likely reason is that its test period had a much lower spike rate than the other symbols.

That made both spike classification and future volatility prediction harder.

Overall takeaway:

predictability was not the same across contracts

the Treasury contracts appeared to provide the clearest microstructure signal

thinner contracts were harder to forecast

records.content:
Financial volatility is not fully stationary.

Volatility levels and spike rates can change over time.

Our pipeline uses several ideas from lecture to make the problem more stable:

Transformation: the regressor uses log1p(...) on the target

Aggregation: raw MBO events are grouped into 1-second buckets

Smoothing: many inputs are rolling 60-second features like rv_past_60 and n_events_60

Differencing: volatility is built from log returns

We do not do full seasonal decomposition.

Instead, we capture intraday patterns with hour_utc.

Overall takeaway:

the market is not fully stationary

the modeling pipeline makes the features more stable

a chronological train/validation/test split is important

hierarchy.lvl1: Intraday Return and Volatility Modeling of Futures Contracts
Intraday Return and Volatility Modeling of Futures Contracts
Intraday Return and Volatility Modeling of Futures Contracts
Intraday Return and Volatility Modeling of Futures Contracts

records.content:
This notebook models intraday dynamics for 9 CME futures contracts across U.S. Treasuries (ZT 2Y, ZF 5Y, TN 10Y, ZN 10Y, ZB 30Y, UB Ultra Bond), energy (BZ Brent Crude, CL WTI Crude), and FX (6E Euro). We fit two classical time-series models to 10-second resampled log returns ŌĆö ARIMA for the mean of returns and GARCH for volatility ŌĆö and see how well they actually work out-of-sample.

We start with ARIMA. Its residual diagnostics (heteroskedasticity, heavy tails, remaining autocorrelation) motivate why we then move to GARCH. Both models are evaluated on a chronological 70/15/15 train/validation/test split.

The data is MBO (market-by-order) trade data stored as parquet in S3, processed through an AWS EMR Spark cluster because the raw dataset is huge (~3M+ rows per ticker). The sample runs from Nov 18, 2025 to Jan 20, 2026.

hierarchy.lvl1: Intraday Return and Volatility Modeling of Futures Contracts
Intraday Return and Volatility Modeling of Futures Contracts
Intraday Return and Volatility Modeling of Futures Contracts
Intraday Return and Volatility Modeling of Futures Contracts

records.content: We work with log returns instead of prices because prices are non-stationary ŌĆö they trend and drift, which breaks the assumptions of most time-series models. Log returns (rŌé£ = log(PŌé£) ŌłÆ log(PŌé£ŌéŗŌéü)) fluctuate around a stable mean near zero, are additive over time, and approximate percentage changes for small values. We assume therefore log returns are stationary.

hierarchy.lvl1: Intraday Return and Volatility Modeling of Futures Contracts
Intraday Return and Volatility Modeling of Futures Contracts

records.content: One of the most consistent patterns in financial data is that large moves tend to follow large moves, and quiet periods tend to stay quiet. This is volatility clustering. Even if returns themselves are uncorrelated, their squares are ŌĆö meaning variance isnŌĆÖt constant, it evolves over time. This is called conditional heteroskedasticity, and itŌĆÖs what GARCH is designed to capture. ItŌĆÖs also what makes ARIMA insufficient on its own.

hierarchy.lvl1: Intraday Return and Volatility Modeling of Futures Contracts
Intraday Return and Volatility Modeling of Futures Contracts

records.content: Financial returns have much heavier events in tails than a normal distribution: extreme moves happen way more often than a bell curve predicts.  This shows up as high kurtosis (a Gaussian has kurtosis of 3; we see values from 60 to 5,800 in this data). To deal with this, the GARCH model uses a Student-t distribution for innovations, where the degrees-of-freedom parameter ╬Į controls how heavy the tails are. Lower ╬Į = heavier tails.

records.content:
ARIMA(1,0,1) models whether past returns help predict future returns (chosen via looking at lags in ACF/PACF plots)r_t = c + \phi_1 r_{t-1} + \theta_1 \varepsilon_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t

We use ACF, PACF, Ljung-Box tests, and residual diagnostics to check whether itŌĆÖs capturing the structure in the data.

hierarchy.lvl1: Intraday Return and Volatility Modeling of Futures Contracts
Intraday Return and Volatility Modeling of Futures Contracts

records.content:
GARCH(1,1) lets the variance change over time based on recent shocks and recent variance:\sigma_t^2 = \omega + \alpha_1 \varepsilon_{t-1}^2 + \beta_1 \sigma_{t-1}^2

╬▒Ōéü controls how much a big return spikes next-period variance, ╬▓Ōéü controls how slowly that spike fades. When ╬▒Ōéü + ╬▓Ōéü is close to 1, volatility shocks are very persistent. We use a zero-mean specification (expected return = 0) since ARIMA already showed the constant is insignificant everywhere.

records.content:
For each ticker:

Extract trade data from S3/Spark for the chosen date range

Resample irregular ticks to a 10-second grid using last-price with forward fill

Compute log returns within each session (so overnight gaps donŌĆÖt contaminate the series)

Split chronologically into 70/15/15 train/val/test

ARIMA ŌĆö fit on training, check residual diagnostics, forecast on val and test

GARCH ŌĆö fit on training, check standardized residual diagnostics, produce one-step-ahead volatility forecasts, compare against a constant-volatility baseline using MAE, RMSE, and QLIKE

Some potential use cases could be:

Risk Management - most asset managers are trying to have a certain controlled level of volatility in their portfolios. If they can predict a certain level of volatility in the future, they can decide to allocate to risky assets differently.

Trading Vol - traders or hedge funds can use this strategy to trade volatility, either via options or volatility future.

Timing of hedging activity - if traders want to own hedges or want to put on trades, choosing a time before high volatility would get better execution and potential payout if they were to buy options.

Together, these plots provide a view of:

how the ticker price evolves during the trading day

how returns fluctuate at a high frequency

how intraday behavior differs across sessions

from pyspark.sql import functions as F
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def plot_ticker(mbo_spark_df, ticker, start_date, end_date):

    base_df = (
        mbo_spark_df
        .filter(
            (F.col("symbol") == ticker) &
            (F.col("session_date") >= F.lit(start_date)) &
            (F.col("session_date") <= F.lit(end_date))
        )
        .select(
            "session_date",
            "second_bucket",
            "filled_price",
            "log_return"
        )
        .orderBy("session_date", "second_bucket")
    )

    # 10-second down sampling
    base_df = base_df.filter((F.second("second_bucket") % 10) == 0)
    price_df = (
        base_df
        .where(F.col("filled_price").isNotNull())
        .select("session_date", "second_bucket", "filled_price")
    )

    ret_df = (
        base_df
        .where(F.col("log_return").isNotNull())
        .select("session_date", "second_bucket", "log_return")
    )

# change to pandas
    pdf_price = price_df.toPandas()
    pdf_ret = ret_df.toPandas()

    if pdf_price.empty and pdf_ret.empty:
        print(f"No data for {ticker}")
        return

  #datetime conversion
    for df in [pdf_price, pdf_ret]:
        if not df.empty:
            df["second_bucket"] = pd.to_datetime(df["second_bucket"])
            df["session_date"] = pd.to_datetime(df["session_date"])

  # plot price
    if not pdf_price.empty:
        plt.figure(figsize=(14, 5))
        for session, g in pdf_price.groupby("session_date"):
            g = g.sort_values("second_bucket")
            plt.plot(g["second_bucket"], g["filled_price"], linewidth=1, label=str(session.date()))

        plt.title(f"{ticker} Price")
        plt.xlabel("Time")
        plt.ylabel("Filled Price")
        plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
        plt.legend(ncol=2, fontsize=8)
        plt.tight_layout()
        plt.show()

# plot log returns
    if not pdf_ret.empty:
        plt.figure(figsize=(14, 5))
        for session, g in pdf_ret.groupby("session_date"):
            g = g.sort_values("second_bucket")
            plt.plot(g["second_bucket"], g["log_return"], linewidth=1, label=str(session.date()))

        plt.title(f"{ticker} Log Return")
        plt.xlabel("Time")
        plt.ylabel("Log Return")
        plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
        plt.legend(ncol=2, fontsize=8)
        plt.tight_layout()
        plt.show()




tickers = tickers = [
    "TN.c.0",
    "BZ.c.0",
    "ZF.c.0",
    "ZT.c.0",
    "CL.c.0",
    "ZN.c.0",
    "ZB.c.0",
    "6E.c.0",
    "UB.c.0"
]

for t in tickers:
    print(f"Processing {t}")
    plot_ticker(mbo_spark_df, t, "2025-11-18", "2026-01-20")

records.content:
We use ARIMA as a baseline model for the mean behavior of intraday log returns. The goal is not to fully model volatility, but to check whether there is any short-run structure in the returns that can be captured with a simple time-series model.

For each ticker, the return series is split in chronological order into training, validation, and test sets. An ARIMA(1,0,1) model is fitted on the training data, then used to generate forecasts on the validation set. We chose this because looking at the lags, usually 1 is significant in autocorrelation.

This setup lets us measure how well a basic linear model explains the return dynamics before moving to volatility-specific models. In most cases, ARIMA captures only limited dependence in returns, while the residuals still show changing variance and heavy tails. That is why ARIMA is treated here as a benchmark for the conditional mean, not as the final model for the full series behavior.

from pyspark.sql import functions as F
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

from statsmodels.tsa.arima.model import ARIMA
from statsmodels.graphics.tsaplots import plot_acf, plot_pacf
from statsmodels.stats.diagnostic import acorr_ljungbox

def run_arima_one_ticker(
    mbo_spark_df,
    ticker="CL.c.0",
    start_date="2025-11-18",
    end_date="2026-01-20",
    freq="10s",
    order=(1, 0, 1),
    single_session_only=None,
    min_points_per_session=30
):
    df = (
        mbo_spark_df
        .filter(
            (F.col("symbol") == ticker) &
            (F.col("session_date") >= F.lit(start_date)) &
            (F.col("session_date") <= F.lit(end_date))
        )
        .select("session_date", "second_bucket", "filled_price")
        .where(F.col("second_bucket").isNotNull())
        .orderBy("session_date", "second_bucket")
    )

    if single_session_only is not None:
        df = df.filter(F.col("session_date") == F.lit(single_session_only))

    pdf = df.toPandas()

    if pdf.empty:
        raise ValueError(f"No data for {ticker} between {start_date} and {end_date}")

    pdf["session_date"] = pd.to_datetime(pdf["session_date"])
    pdf["second_bucket"] = pd.to_datetime(pdf["second_bucket"])
    pdf = pdf.sort_values(["session_date", "second_bucket"]).reset_index(drop=True)

    print(f"{ticker} | rows: {len(pdf)}")
    print("Sessions:", sorted(pdf["session_date"].dt.strftime("%Y-%m-%d").unique()))

    # Resample per session
    session_frames = []

    for session, g in pdf.groupby("session_date"):
        g = g.sort_values("second_bucket").dropna(subset=["filled_price"])

        if g.empty:
            continue

        g = g.set_index("second_bucket")

        s = g["filled_price"].resample(freq).last()
        s = s.ffill().dropna()

        if len(s) < min_points_per_session:
            continue

        tmp = s.to_frame("filled_price").reset_index()
        tmp["session_date"] = pd.to_datetime(session)
        session_frames.append(tmp)

    if not session_frames:
        raise ValueError("No sessions")

    ts_df = pd.concat(session_frames, ignore_index=True)
    ts_df = ts_df.sort_values(["session_date", "second_bucket"]).reset_index(drop=True)


    ts_df["log_price"] = np.log(ts_df["filled_price"])
    ts_df["log_return"] = ts_df.groupby("session_date")["log_price"].diff()

    model_df = ts_df.dropna(subset=["log_return"]).copy()

    series = model_df.set_index("second_bucket")["log_return"]
    series = series[~series.index.duplicated()]

    if len(series) < 100:
        raise ValueError("No data")

    print(f"Resampled points: {len(series)}")

    # Train / val / test split 70/15/15
    n = len(series)
    train_end = int(n * 0.70)
    val_end = int(n * 0.85)

    train = series.iloc[:train_end]
    val = series.iloc[train_end:val_end]
    test = series.iloc[val_end:]

    print(f"Train: {len(train)} | Val: {len(val)} | Test: {len(test)}")

    # Plots
    plt.figure(figsize=(14, 4))
    plt.plot(series, linewidth=1)
    plt.title(f"{ticker} log return")
    plt.grid(alpha=0.3)
    plt.show()

    plt.figure(figsize=(14, 4))
    plt.plot(train, linewidth=1)
    plt.title("Training log return")
    plt.grid(alpha=0.3)
    plt.show()

    plot_acf(train.dropna(), lags=50)
    plt.show()

    plot_pacf(train.dropna(), lags=50, method="ywm")
    plt.show()

    # Fit on training set
    model_train = ARIMA(train, order=order)
    results_train = model_train.fit()

    print(results_train.summary())

    # residuals
    resid = results_train.resid.dropna()

    plt.figure(figsize=(14, 4))
    plt.plot(resid, linewidth=1)
    plt.title("Residuals (train)")
    plt.grid(alpha=0.3)
    plt.show()

    plot_acf(resid, lags=50)
    plt.show()

    lb = acorr_ljungbox(resid, lags=[10, 20, 30], return_df=True)
    print("\nLjung-Box:\n", lb)

    # Validation forecast
    val_forecast = results_train.get_forecast(steps=len(val))
    val_pred = val_forecast.predicted_mean
    val_ci = val_forecast.conf_int()

    val_pred.index = val.index
    val_ci.index = val.index

    val_mae = np.mean(np.abs(val - val_pred))
    val_rmse = np.sqrt(np.mean((val - val_pred) ** 2))

    plt.figure(figsize=(14, 5))
    plt.plot(train, label="Train")
    plt.plot(val, label="Val actual")
    plt.plot(val_pred, label="Val pred")

    plt.fill_between(
        val_ci.index,
        val_ci.iloc[:, 0],
        val_ci.iloc[:, 1],
        alpha=0.2
    )

    plt.legend()
    plt.title("Validation forecast: log returns")
    plt.grid(alpha=0.3)
    plt.show()

    print(f"\nVal MAE: {val_mae:.6f} | RMSE: {val_rmse:.6f}")

    # Refit on training + validation
    train_val = series.iloc[:val_end]
    results_train_val = ARIMA(train_val, order=order).fit()

    # Test forecast
    test_forecast = results_train_val.get_forecast(steps=len(test))
    test_pred = test_forecast.predicted_mean
    test_ci = test_forecast.conf_int()

    test_pred.index = test.index
    test_ci.index = test.index

    test_mae = np.mean(np.abs(test - test_pred))
    test_rmse = np.sqrt(np.mean((test - test_pred) ** 2))

    plt.figure(figsize=(14, 5))
    plt.plot(train_val, label="Train+Val")
    plt.plot(test, label="Test actual")
    plt.plot(test_pred, label="Test pred")

    plt.fill_between(
        test_ci.index,
        test_ci.iloc[:, 0],
        test_ci.iloc[:, 1],
        alpha=0.2
    )

    plt.legend()
    plt.title("Test forecast: log returns")
    plt.grid(alpha=0.3)
    plt.show()

    print(f"\nTest MAE: {test_mae:.6f} | RMSE: {test_rmse:.6f}")

    return {
        "ticker": ticker,
        "order": order,
        "ts_df": ts_df,
        "model_df": model_df,
        "series_log_return": series,
        "train": train,
        "val": val,
        "test": test,
        "results_train": results_train,
        "results_train_val": results_train_val,
        "val_forecast_return": val_pred,
        "test_forecast_return": test_pred,
        "val_mae": val_mae,
        "val_rmse": val_rmse,
        "test_mae": test_mae,
        "test_rmse": test_rmse,
        "ljung_box": lb,
    }




tickers = tickers = [
    "TN.c.0",
    "BZ.c.0",
    "ZF.c.0",
    "ZT.c.0",
    "CL.c.0",
    "ZN.c.0",
    "ZB.c.0",
    "6E.c.0",
    "UB.c.0"
]
results = {}

for ticker in tickers:
    print(f"\n Running ARIMA for ticker: {ticker} ")
    try:
        out = run_arima_one_ticker(
            mbo_spark_df=mbo_spark_df,
            ticker=ticker,
            start_date="2025-11-18",
            end_date="2026-01-20",
            freq="10s",
            order=(1, 0, 1),
            single_session_only=None
        )
        results[ticker] = out
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Failed for {ticker}: {e}")

hierarchy.lvl1: Intraday Return and Volatility Modeling of Futures Contracts
Intraday Return and Volatility Modeling of Futures Contracts

records.content:
We fit the same ARIMA(1,0,1) model to every ticker to keep the mean-model comparison consistent across assets. A few patterns show up pretty clearly.

First, the constant is not significant for any of the tickers, which is what we would expect at a 10-second frequency since the average log return is basically zero. The AR(1) and MA(1) terms are usually significant, but in a lot of cases they offset each other quite a bit. For example, TN has AR = 0.38 and MA = -0.50, while BZ has AR = -0.54 and MA = 0.53. That kind of near-cancellation looks more like microstructure effects, like bid-ask bounce, than real predictability.

The residual diagnostics are still pretty bad. The Ljung-Box results at lags 10, 20, and 30 are significant for most of the tickers, so there is still serial dependence left in the residuals. TN, ZF, and ZN are especially clear examples. BZ and CL look a little better than the others, but overall the ARIMA model is not fully cleaning up the dependence in the series.

The residuals are also very far from normal. Kurtosis is huge across all tickers, from about 62 for CL up to more than 5,800 for ZT. That is not surprising for high-frequency data, but it does show that a constant-variance Gaussian-type setup is a bad fit here. For ZT in particular, the extreme kurtosis probably comes from a lot of zero returns mixed with occasional jumps.

We also see strong evidence of heteroskedasticity. The Prob(H) values are essentially 0 for every ticker, which means the variance is changing over time and ARIMA is not built to handle that.

There are also some numerical issues in the fits. Every model gave a maximum likelihood convergence warning, and several of the covariance matrices are singular or close to singular. So while the coefficient estimates themselves may still be usable as rough signals, we should not put too much weight on the reported standard errors or z-statistics.

In terms of forecasting, the validation and test MAE/RMSE values are all very small in absolute size, but that is mostly because 10-second returns are themselves extremely close to zero. In practice, the forecasts flatten out quickly and mostly predict something close to zero. So ARIMA does not add much for multi-step forecasting at this frequency.

A few ticker-specific differences also stand out. Energy contracts like CL and BZ have the largest forecast errors, which matches their higher volatility. Shorter-duration Treasuries like ZT and ZF have the smallest errors, which fits their lower per-tick volatility. 6E also has an extremely small MAE, but that mostly reflects how often the return is near zero rather than strong model performance.

Overall, ARIMA(1,0,1) works better here as a simple benchmark for the conditional mean than as a full model for the series. The remaining autocorrelation, heavy tails, and changing variance all point toward volatility models as the more important next step.

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.gridspec as gridspec
from matplotlib.lines import Line2D
from scipy.stats import norm

rows = []
for ticker, r in results.items():
    res = r["results_train"]
    params = res.params
    diag = res.test_heteroskedasticity("breakvar")

    rows.append({
        "ticker": ticker,
        "n_train": len(r["train"]),
        "ar1": params.get("ar.L1", np.nan),
        "ma1": params.get("ma.L1", np.nan),
        "sigma2": params.get("sigma2", np.nan),
        "aic": res.aic,
        "bic": res.bic,
        "log_lik": res.llf,
        "ljung_box_10_stat": r["ljung_box"].loc[10, "lb_stat"],
        "ljung_box_10_p": r["ljung_box"].loc[10, "lb_pvalue"],
        "val_mae": r["val_mae"],
        "val_rmse": r["val_rmse"],
        "test_mae": r["test_mae"],
        "test_rmse": r["test_rmse"],
        "kurtosis": r["results_train"].resid.kurtosis(),
        "skewness": r["results_train"].resid.skew(),
        "het_H": diag[0][0],
        "het_p": diag[0][1],
        "train_std": r["train"].std(),
        "test_std": r["test"].std(),
    })

summary = pd.DataFrame(rows).set_index("ticker")

order = ["ZT.c.0", "ZF.c.0", "TN.c.0", "ZN.c.0", "ZB.c.0", "UB.c.0", "BZ.c.0", "CL.c.0", "6E.c.0"]
order = [t for t in order if t in summary.index]
summary = summary.loc[order]

print(summary.to_string())

COLORS = {
    "ZT.c.0": "#1f77b4", "ZF.c.0": "#ff7f0e", "TN.c.0": "#2ca02c",
    "ZN.c.0": "#d62728", "ZB.c.0": "#9467bd", "UB.c.0": "#8c564b",
    "BZ.c.0": "#e377c2", "CL.c.0": "#7f7f7f", "6E.c.0": "#bcbd22",
}

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 5), sharey=False)

x = np.arange(len(summary))
w = 0.35

axes[0].bar(x - w/2, summary["val_mae"] * 1e4, w, label="Val MAE", color="#5b9bd5")
axes[0].bar(x + w/2, summary["test_mae"] * 1e4, w, label="Test MAE", color="#ed7d31")
axes[0].set_xticks(x)
axes[0].set_xticklabels(summary.index, rotation=45, ha="right")
axes[0].set_ylabel("MAE (├Ś10Ōü╗Ōü┤)")
axes[0].set_title("Mean Absolute Error by Ticker")
axes[0].legend()
axes[0].grid(axis="y", alpha=0.3)

axes[1].bar(x - w/2, summary["val_rmse"] * 1e4, w, label="Val RMSE", color="#5b9bd5")
axes[1].bar(x + w/2, summary["test_rmse"] * 1e4, w, label="Test RMSE", color="#ed7d31")

axes[0].bar(x, summary["kurtosis"], color=[COLORS.get(t, "#999") for t in summary.index])
axes[0].set_yscale("log")

colors_skew = ["#d62728" if s < 0 else "#2ca02c" for s in summary["skewness"]]
axes[1].bar(x, summary["skewness"], color=colors_skew)
axes[1].axhline(0, color="black", linewidth=0.8)
axes[1].set_xticks(x)
axes[1].set_xticklabels(summary.index, rotation=45, ha="right")
axes[1].set_ylabel("Skewness")
axes[1].set_title("Residual Skewness")
axes[1].grid(axis="y", alpha=0.3)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 5))

ax.bar(x, summary["het_H"], color=[COLORS.get(t, "#999") for t in summary.index])
ax.axhline(1.0, color="red", linestyle="--", linewidth=1, label="H = 1")
ax.set_xticks(x)
ax.set_xticklabels(summary.index, rotation=45, ha="right")
ax.set_ylabel("H statistic")
ax.set_title("Heteroskedasticity Test")
ax.legend()
ax.grid(axis="y", alpha=0.3)

for i, v in enumerate(summary["het_H"]):
    ax.text(i, v + 0.01, f"{v:.2f}", ha="center", fontsize=9)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 5))

ax.bar(x, summary["ljung_box_10_stat"], color=[COLORS.get(t, "#999") for t in summary.index])
ax.set_xticks(x)
ax.set_xticklabels(summary.index, rotation=45, ha="right")
ax.set_ylabel("Ljung-Box Q statistic (lag 10)")
ax.set_title("Residual Autocorrelation")
ax.grid(axis="y", alpha=0.3)

for i, (stat, pv) in enumerate(zip(summary["ljung_box_10_stat"], summary["ljung_box_10_p"])):
    label = f"p={pv:.1e}" if pv < 0.001 else f"p={pv:.3f}"
    ax.text(i, stat + 2, label, ha="center", fontsize=8, rotation=45)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 6))

for ticker in summary.index:
    resid = results[ticker]["results_train"].resid.dropna()
    normed = (resid - resid.mean()) / resid.std()
    normed = normed[(normed > -6) & (normed < 6)]
    normed.plot.kde(ax=ax, label=ticker, color=COLORS.get(ticker, "#999"), linewidth=1.2)

xg = np.linspace(-6, 6, 300)
ax.plot(xg, norm.pdf(xg), "k--", linewidth=1.5, label="N(0,1)")
ax.set_xlim(-6, 6)
ax.set_title("Standardized Residual Distributions (KDE) vs Gaussian")
ax.set_xlabel("Standardized residual")
ax.legend(fontsize=8)
ax.grid(alpha=0.3)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(14, 6))

window = 5000

for ticker in summary.index:
    s = results[ticker]["series_log_return"]
    roll = s.rolling(window).std()
    roll_thin = roll.iloc[::500]
    ax.plot(roll_thin.values, label=ticker, color=COLORS.get(ticker, "#999"), linewidth=1, alpha=0.85)

ax.set_title(f"Rolling Volatility (window = {window} obs Ōēł {window*10//3600}h)")
ax.set_ylabel("Rolling Žā of log returns")
ax.set_xlabel("Observation index (thinned)")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, ncol=3)
ax.grid(alpha=0.3)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 6))

for ticker in summary.index:
    ax.scatter(
        summary.loc[ticker, "train_std"] * 1e4,
        summary.loc[ticker, "test_rmse"] * 1e4,
        s=120,
        color=COLORS.get(ticker, "#999"),
        zorder=5,
        edgecolors="black",
        linewidth=0.5
    )
    ax.annotate(
        ticker,
        (summary.loc[ticker, "train_std"] * 1e4, summary.loc[ticker, "test_rmse"] * 1e4),
        textcoords="offset points",
        xytext=(8, 4),
        fontsize=9
    )

xs = summary["train_std"] * 1e4
ys = summary["test_rmse"] * 1e4
m, b = np.polyfit(xs, ys, 1)
ax.plot(np.sort(xs), m * np.sort(xs) + b, "k--", linewidth=0.8, alpha=0.6)

ax.set_xlabel("Training Žā (├Ś10Ōü╗Ōü┤)")
ax.set_ylabel("Test RMSE (├Ś10Ōü╗Ōü┤)")
ax.set_title("Forecast Error vs Underlying Volatility")
ax.grid(alpha=0.3)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

heat_cols = ["ar1", "ma1", "het_H", "kurtosis", "skewness",
             "ljung_box_10_stat", "val_mae", "test_mae", "test_rmse"]

heat = summary[heat_cols].copy()
heat_norm = (heat - heat.min()) / (heat.max() - heat.min() + 1e-15)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(14, 5))

im = ax.imshow(heat_norm.values, aspect="auto", cmap="RdYlGn_r")

ax.set_xticks(range(len(heat_cols)))
ax.set_xticklabels([c.replace("_", "\n") for c in heat_cols], fontsize=9)
ax.set_yticks(range(len(heat.index)))
ax.set_yticklabels(heat.index, fontsize=10)

for i in range(len(heat.index)):
    for j in range(len(heat_cols)):
        val = heat.iloc[i, j]
        txt = f"{val:.4f}" if abs(val) < 1 else f"{val:.1f}"
        ax.text(
            j, i, txt,
            ha="center", va="center", fontsize=7.5,
            color="white" if heat_norm.iloc[i, j] > 0.6 else "black"
        )

ax.set_title("Cross-Ticker Comparison Heatmap")
plt.colorbar(im, ax=ax, shrink=0.8)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

print("\n" + "=" * 90)
print("SUMMARY TABLE")
print("=" * 90)

display_cols = ["ar1", "ma1", "het_H", "kurtosis", "skewness",
                "ljung_box_10_stat", "val_mae", "test_mae", "test_rmse"]

fmt = summary[display_cols].copy()
fmt["val_mae"] = fmt["val_mae"].map(lambda x: f"{x:.6f}")
fmt["test_mae"] = fmt["test_mae"].map(lambda x: f"{x:.6f}")
